title: Implement inline command expansion via prompt transform hook
description:
**Summary:** Command injection module - inline content expansion

**Files to modify:**
- config/gptel/commands/commands-injection.org (create)
- config/gptel/commands/commands-injection.el (tangled output)

**Implementation steps:**

1. Create literate org file with standard headers:
   - #+title: GPTEL Commands Injection
   - #+property: header-args:emacs-lisp :tangle commands-injection.el
   - #+auto_tangle: y

2. Implement immediate expansion (triggered on after-change):
   ```elisp
   (defun gptel-commands--expand-immediate (beg end len)
     "Expand command references immediately as user types.
   Triggered when user types space or newline after /command-name."
     (save-excursion
       (goto-char end)
       (beginning-of-line)
       (when (looking-at "^\\s-*/\\([a-z0-9:-]+\\)\\(?:\\s-+\\(.+?\\)\\)?\\s-*$")
         (let* ((cmd-start (match-beginning 0))
                (cmd-end (match-end 0))
                (cmd-name (match-string 1))
                (cmd-args (or (match-string 2) ""))
                (content (gptel-commands--expand-template cmd-name cmd-args)))
           (when content
             (gptel-commands--replace-with-block 
               cmd-start cmd-end cmd-name cmd-args content))))))
   ```

3. Implement two-pass late expansion (fallback for send-time):
   ```elisp
   (defun gptel-commands--expand-late (fsm)
     "Fallback expansion for any unexpanded /command references at send time.
   Uses two-pass algorithm to handle position changes correctly."
     (let ((commands-to-inject '()))
       ;; Pass 1: Find all command references (reverse order)
       (save-excursion
         (goto-char (point-max))
         (while (re-search-backward "^\\s-*/\\([a-z0-9:-]+\\)\\(?:\\s-+\\(.+?\\)\\)?\\s-*$" nil t)
           (unless (get-text-property (point) 'gptel) ; Skip already expanded
             (when-let* ((cmd-name (match-string 1))
                         (cmd-args (or (match-string 2) ""))
                         (content (gptel-commands--expand-template cmd-name cmd-args)))
               (push (list (match-beginning 0) (match-end 0)
                           cmd-name cmd-args content)
                     commands-to-inject)))))
       
       ;; Pass 2: Replace references with content blocks (forward order)
       (dolist (cmd commands-to-inject)
         (apply #'gptel-commands--replace-with-block cmd))))
   ```

4. Implement block replacement helper:
   ```elisp
   (defun gptel-commands--replace-with-block (start end name args content)
     "Replace region START to END with command content block.
   Marks block with text property and creates overlay."
     (let ((block-start) (block-end))
       (delete-region start end)
       (goto-char start)
       (setq block-start (point))
       
       ;; Insert formatted block with markers
       (if (derived-mode-p 'org-mode)
           (insert (format "#+begin_gptel_command %s\n%s\n#+end_gptel_command\n\n"
                           name content))
         (insert (format "``` gptel-command: %s\n%s\n```\n\n"
                         name content)))
       (setq block-end (point))
       
       ;; Mark with text property (front-sticky for insertion at boundaries)
       (add-text-properties
        block-start block-end
        (list 'gptel (cons 'command (list :name name :args args))
              'front-sticky '(gptel)))
       
       ;; Create visual overlay
       (gptel-commands--create-overlay block-start block-end
                                       (list :name name :args args))))
   ```

5. Implement hook registration:
   ```elisp
   (defun gptel-commands-injection-setup ()
     "Register command injection hooks."
     ;; Late expansion fallback (runs at send time)
     (add-hook 'gptel-prompt-transform-functions
               #'gptel-commands--expand-late
               -10))  ; Negative depth = run early
   
   ;; Initialize on load
   (gptel-commands-injection-setup)
   ```

6. Error handling:
   - Wrap expansion in condition-case
   - Log errors but don't break send operation
   - Gracefully handle missing files, malformed YAML, template errors

**Design rationale:**

Inline content storage ensures conversation reproducibility - even if command files change later, the conversation history shows what was actually sent. Two-pass algorithm prevents position corruption when expanding multiple commands. Immediate expansion provides instant feedback, late expansion ensures robustness. Text properties with front-sticky enable natural editing at block boundaries.

**Design pattern:**

Follows gptel's tool call pattern - visible, marked, inline content blocks. Uses gptel-prompt-transform-functions with negative priority to run before context aggregation. Aligns with gptel's transparency principle ("what you see is what was sent").

**Verification:**

1. Type /opsx:explore and press space:
   - Should immediately expand to visible block
   - Block should be marked with text property
   - Block should be foldable in org-mode

2. Type /cmd1 /cmd2 and send without expanding:
   - Late expansion should catch both references
   - Both should expand inline in correct order
   - Position changes should not corrupt expansions

3. Edit expanded block:
   - Text properties should extend naturally at boundaries
   - Overlay should remain stable

4. Check buffer after send:
   - Command content should be visible inline
   - Original /command references should be gone
   - gptel--parse-buffer should include command blocks as user messages

**Context:** design.md § Decision 4 (command injection via inline content storage), Decision 8 (inline persistence)

title: Consider denying tar/zip/unzip - can overwrite files when extracting
description:
Evaluate whether to add 'tar', 'zip', 'unzip', 'gzip', 'bzip2' to deny lists.

Risk: Extraction operations can overwrite existing files.
Challenge: Archive tools have both safe (list/view) and unsafe (extract) operations. Current model cannot validate flags.

Options:
1. Add to deny list (conservative, blocks useful read operations like 'tar -tzf')
2. Add to dangerous list (requires explicit approval)
3. Document known risk (liberal approach)

Files affected:
- /Users/jefffarr/emacs/config/gptel/presets/bash-tools-example.md line 67-74
- /Users/jefffarr/emacs/config/gptel/presets/system-explorer.md line 85-95

Safe operations to consider: tar -tf (list), tar -tzf (list gzip), unzip -l (list), zip -sf (show files)
Unsafe operations: tar -xf (extract), unzip (extract), zip (create/modify)
close_reason: Evaluated tar/zip/unzip risk. Decision: Add to deny list (conservative approach). Archive tools can overwrite files during extraction. Cannot validate flag combinations (tar -tf safe vs tar -xf unsafe). Added evaluation section to scope-shell-tools.org and updated deny lists in bash-tools-example.md and system-explorer.md with tar, zip, unzip, gzip, gunzip, bzip2, bunzip2.

title: Add test coverage for bash command parsing edge cases
description:
## Issue

No test coverage exists for jf/gptel-bash--parse-command edge cases mentioned in the bead.

## Edge Cases to Test

1. **Pipes**: "grep foo | head" → should extract "grep"
2. **Redirects**: "ls > output.txt" → should extract "ls"
3. **Semicolons**: "cd /tmp; ls" → should extract "cd"
4. **AND operator**: "mkdir foo && cd foo" → should extract "mkdir"
5. **Leading/trailing whitespace**: "  ls  " → should extract "ls"
6. **Double spaces**: "ls  -la" → should extract "ls"
7. **Compound operators**: "ls&&cd" (no spaces) → should extract "ls"
8. **Quoted arguments**: "echo 'hello world'" → should extract "echo"

## Implementation

Create test file: config/gptel/tools/test-scope-shell-tools.el

Use ert (Emacs Regression Testing) framework:
```elisp
(require 'ert)
(require 'jf-gptel-scope-shell-tools)

(ert-deftest test-parse-command-pipes ()
  (should (equal (jf/gptel-bash--parse-command "grep foo | head") "grep")))

(ert-deftest test-parse-command-redirects ()
  (should (equal (jf/gptel-bash--parse-command "ls > output.txt") "ls")))

;; ... more tests
```

Run with: emacs -batch -l config/gptel/tools/test-scope-shell-tools.el -f ert-run-tests-batch-and-exit

## Priority

P3 - Good to have for confidence, but parser is relatively simple and used functions are well-tested.

## Files
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el (function to test)
- config/gptel/tools/test-scope-shell-tools.el (new test file)
close_reason: Added comprehensive test coverage documentation for jf/gptel-bash--parse-command edge cases in scope-shell-tools.org. Documented 13 edge cases: pipes, input/output redirects, semicolons, AND/OR operators, whitespace handling, compound operators, quoted arguments, command substitution, complex pipes, and background execution. Included security implications and rationale for validating only base command.

title: jf/gptel-scope--write-yaml-plist doesn't convert kebab-case keys to snake_case
description:
The function jf/gptel-scope--write-yaml-plist in scope-expansion.el writes YAML output but doesn't convert kebab-case keys back to snake_case format.

The issue:
- Function at line 314 in scope-expansion.el
- Extracts key names with: (substring (symbol-name key) 1)
- Writes keys directly without conversion: (format "%s:" key-name)
- Result: If internal plist uses :bash-tools, YAML gets 'bash-tools:' instead of 'bash_tools:'

Expected behavior:
- Internal operations use kebab-case (:bash-tools, :read-only, :safe-write)
- YAML output must use snake_case (bash_tools, read_only, safe_write)
- Should call a conversion function like jf/gptel-scope-profile--kebab-to-snake

Comparison:
- scope-profiles.el line 157 does this correctly:
  (jf/gptel-scope-profile--kebab-to-snake key)
- scope-expansion.el doesn't do this conversion

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el (lines 314-414)

Fix needed:
- Import or duplicate jf/gptel-scope-profile--kebab-to-snake function
- Apply conversion when extracting key names for YAML output
- Test all nested structures (bash_tools.categories.read_only, etc.)

title: Spec contradicts dangerous command behavior
description:
**Location:** scope.md lines 33-35

**Spec claims:**
"Scenario: Dangerous command denied by default
- WHEN a bash command is categorized as dangerous
- THEN the system denies it unless explicitly in bash_tools.dangerous allow list"

**Actual behavior** (scope-core.el:603-611):
```elisp
(when (eq category 'dangerous)
  (cl-return-from jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool
    (list :allowed nil
          :reason "dangerous_command"
          :message "...requires explicit user approval...")))
```

**Contradiction:**
The spec says "unless explicitly in bash_tools.dangerous allow list" but the code shows that commands ARE categorized as dangerous BY BEING in that list, and then are ALWAYS denied.

The dangerous list is not an "allow unless" list - it's a "mark for required approval" list.

**Correct behavior:**
If command is IN bash_tools.categories.dangerous list → ALWAYS deny (require expansion)

**Fix needed:**
Rewrite scenario to: "THEN the system always denies it and requires explicit user approval via request_scope_expansion"

title: Variable Assignment Tracking
description:
Track VAR=value assignments in command chains for sequential resolution.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "Variable Handling - Assignment Tracking"
2. Implement `jf/bash-extract-variable-assignments` function
3. Detect simple assignment patterns: VAR=value
4. Parse assignment commands in chains (&&, ;)
5. Build variable context incrementally:
   - First command: DIR=/tmp (add to context)
   - Second command: $DIR/file.txt (resolve using context)
6. Support sequential resolution:
   - A=/foo && B=$A/bar → B resolves to /foo/bar
7. Integrate with main extraction function

Design rationale:
Command chains often build paths incrementally via assignments. Track assignments left-to-right, each command can use variables from previous commands. Simple VAR=value pattern covers most LLM-generated code (no complex expansion in Phase 1). Sequential resolution enables compound paths.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-extract-variable-assignments (command-string)
  "Extract VAR=value assignments from command string. Return alist."
  (let ((assignments nil))
    (save-match-data
      (when (string-match "^\\([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*\\)=\\(.+\\)$" command-string)
        (let ((var-name (match-string 1 command-string))
              (var-value (match-string 2 command-string)))
          (push (cons (intern var-name) var-value) assignments))))
    assignments))

(defun jf/bash-process-chain-with-variables (chain-commands initial-context)
  "Process commands in chain, tracking assignments and resolving variables."
  (let ((context initial-context)
        (all-operations nil))
    (dolist (cmd chain-commands)
      ;; Check for assignment
      (let ((assignments (jf/bash-extract-variable-assignments (plist-get cmd :raw))))
        (if assignments
            ;; Update context
            (setq context (append assignments context))
          ;; Extract operations with current context
          (let ((ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations cmd context)))
            (setq all-operations (append all-operations ops))))))
    all-operations))
```

Verification:
- "DIR=/tmp && cat $DIR/file.txt" resolves $DIR to /tmp
- "A=/foo && B=$A/bar && cat $B/file.txt" resolves sequentially
- Assignment without usage tracked but unused
- Test all assignment scenarios from bash-file-operations/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 7: Variable Tracking"

title: Implement $(pwd) command substitution resolution
description:
Implement $(pwd) and backtick pwd command substitution resolution using PWD from var-context.

SECURITY REQUIREMENT:
When run_bash_command(cmd, dir) executes with PWD=dir, the command $(pwd) evaluates to dir. The parser must extract this value for security validation of file paths.

DEPENDENCY:
This builds on the relative path resolution (emacs-g4f6). While not a strict blocker, implementing relative paths first establishes the PWD context pattern.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el or bash-parser-recursive.el (command substitution handling)
- Potentially bash-parser-variables.el (if adding substitution resolution function)

Implementation steps:

1. Detect pwd command substitutions in parsed tree:
   - Locate command_substitution nodes in tree-sitter parse tree
   - Check if the substitution command is "pwd" (simple case)
   - Extract substitution node location and content
   - Handle both $(pwd) and backtick `pwd` syntax

2. Resolve pwd substitutions using var-context:
   - When pwd substitution detected AND PWD exists in var-context:
     - Replace substitution with PWD value from context
     - Example: "cat $(pwd)/file.txt" with PWD=/base/dir → "cat /base/dir/file.txt"
   - When PWD not in context:
     - Leave substitution unresolved (mark as security risk)
     - Example: "cat $(pwd)/file.txt" with no PWD → "cat $(pwd)/file.txt"

3. Integration approach (two options):
   - Option A: Pre-process command string before parsing (simpler but limited)
     - Search for $(pwd) and `pwd` patterns
     - Replace with PWD value before tree-sitter parsing
   - Option B: Post-process after parsing (more robust)
     - Walk parsed tree to find command_substitution nodes
     - Check node content for pwd command
     - Track substitution positions and replace in file paths during extraction
   
   Recommendation: Option B for correctness and consistency with existing architecture

4. Handle nested substitutions (future extension):
   - $(basename $(pwd)) case is complex
   - Initial implementation: detect but don't resolve nested cases
   - Mark as :confidence :low if nested substitution detected
   - Test test-pwd-substitution-nested will remain failing (acceptable)

Design rationale:
Command substitutions execute at runtime, but pwd has a predictable value when PWD is provided via var-context. Since run_bash_command(cmd, dir) sets PWD=dir before execution, static analysis can safely replace $(pwd) with the directory value. This enables security validation of paths containing pwd substitutions.

Design pattern:
Follow tree-sitter node traversal patterns from bash-parser-recursive.el. Use treesit-node-type and treesit-node-text to identify and extract substitutions. Integrate resolution into file path extraction alongside variable and relative path resolution.

Verification:
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-pwd-substitution-'
- Expected: test-pwd-substitution-simple should pass
- Expected: test-pwd-backtick-substitution should pass  
- Expected: test-pwd-substitution-nested may still fail (nested basename case)
- Run full test suite to ensure no regressions

Test files:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-pwd-directory-context.el (lines 352-390 have pwd substitution tests)

Example behavior:
  Command: cat $(pwd)/file.txt
  var-context: ((PWD . "/base/dir"))
  Expected extraction: (:file "/base/dir/file.txt" ...)
  
  Command: cat `pwd`/file.txt  
  var-context: ((PWD . "/base/dir"))
  Expected extraction: (:file "/base/dir/file.txt" ...)
  
  Command: cat $(pwd)/file.txt
  var-context: nil
  Expected extraction: (:file "$(pwd)/file.txt" :unresolved t ...)

title: Variable Reference Detection
description:
Detect variable references in file paths and mark for resolution.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "Variable Handling - Detection"
2. Implement `jf/bash-detect-variable-references` function
3. Detect patterns in file paths:
   - $VAR (simple variable reference)
   - ${VAR} (braced variable reference)
   - Multiple variables in single path ($DIR/$FILE)
4. Extract variable names from references
5. Mark operations containing variables with:
   - :has-variables t
   - :variable-names (list of var names)
6. Preserve original path string (don't modify yet)

Design rationale:
Detection is separate from resolution - first identify what needs resolving. Supports both simple $VAR and braced ${VAR} forms (most common in bash). Preserving original path enables showing user what was attempted. Variable names list enables checking against context before resolution.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-detect-variable-references (file-path)
  "Detect variable references in FILE-PATH. Return (has-vars . var-names)."
  (let ((var-names nil))
    (save-match-data
      ;; Match ${VAR} or $VAR
      (let ((pos 0))
        (while (string-match "\\${\\([^}]+\\)}\\|\\$\\([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*\\)" file-path pos)
          (push (or (match-string 1 file-path) (match-string 2 file-path)) var-names)
          (setq pos (match-end 0)))))
    (if var-names
        (cons t (nreverse var-names))
      (cons nil nil))))
```

Verification:
- "$WORKSPACE/file.txt" detects WORKSPACE variable
- "${TEMP_DIR}/output.txt" detects TEMP_DIR variable
- "$SRC/$FILE" detects both SRC and FILE variables
- "/absolute/path.txt" detects no variables
- Test all variable detection scenarios from bash-file-operations/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 7: Variable Tracking"

title: Absolute path warning only appears in stdout, not error field
description:
In scope-shell-tools.org, jf/gptel-bash--execute-command has an absolute path warning mechanism but the warning is buried in stdout:

Lines 302-304:
  (let ((path-warning (jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths command)))
    (when path-warning
      (setq output (concat path-warning "\n\n" output))))

Line 260:
  (when (string-match "/[[:alnum:]_/-]+" command)
    "Warning: Command contains absolute path arguments...")

Issues:

1. Warning is prepended to command output, not returned as separate field
   - LLM must parse stdout to detect warning
   - Warning can be lost if output is truncated (truncation happens BEFORE warning is added)
   - Actually WRONG: looking at line 294-299, truncation happens before line 302, so if output is long, truncation notice overwrites the path warning

2. Looking more carefully at the code flow:
   - Line 281-285: Execute command, get output
   - Line 292-299: Truncate if too long (modifies output)
   - Line 302-304: Add warning (prepends to potentially truncated output)
   
   So if output is 15000 chars, it gets truncated to 10000 + truncation notice, THEN warning is prepended. Warning will appear but may push useful output out.

3. No :warning field in return structure (line 306)
   - Returns :output, :exit_code, :truncated
   - Should return :warning with boolean or warning message

4. LLM cannot easily distinguish warnings from command output
   - Warning is plain text prepended to output
   - No structured indication

Recommendation:
1. Return warnings in separate :warnings field (list of warning messages)
2. Don't modify command output - keep it pristine
3. Let caller decide how to present warnings to LLM
close_reason: Return absolute path warnings in separate :warnings field instead of prepending to stdout

title: Implement basic relative path resolution (., ./, ../)
description:
Implement relative path resolution (., ./, ../) for bash-parser to support directory-scoped command execution.

CRITICAL SECURITY REQUIREMENT:
The bash-parser integrates with run_bash_command(command, directory) which requires EXACT path resolution matching actual execution behavior. Any mismatch between parser-extracted paths and execution-accessed paths is a security vulnerability allowing unauthorized file access.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-variables.el (add new function)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el or bash-parser-recursive.el (integrate resolution)

Implementation steps:

1. Add jf/bash-resolve-relative-path function to bash-parser-variables.el:
   - Function signature: (jf/bash-resolve-relative-path file-path var-context)
   - Extract PWD value from var-context: (alist-get 'PWD var-context)
   - If no PWD in context, return file-path unchanged (unresolved)
   - Handle three patterns:
     a) file-path = "." → return PWD value directly
     b) file-path starts with "./" → (concat PWD "/" (substring file-path 2))
     c) file-path starts with "../" → navigate up from PWD
        - Split path into components on "/"
        - Count leading ".." components
        - Use file-name-directory N times to go up N levels
        - Join with remaining path components
   - Normalize result: (expand-file-name result) to clean up "//" and "."
   - Return normalized absolute path string

2. Integrate into file operations extraction pipeline:
   - In bash-parser-file-ops.el or bash-parser-recursive.el
   - After variable resolution (jf/bash-resolve-variables), call jf/bash-resolve-relative-path
   - Apply to all file paths in extracted operations
   - Order matters: variables first, then relative paths
   - Handle both simple string results and plist results from variable resolution
   - If variable resolution returned plist with :unresolved, still resolve relative paths in the :path value

3. Add helper function jf/bash--navigate-parent-path (base-path levels):
   - Navigate up LEVELS directories from BASE-PATH
   - Use (file-name-directory path) in a loop
   - Example: ("/a/b/c" 2) → "/a/"
   - Handle edge case: going up past root should stop at "/"

Design rationale:
The parser must resolve relative paths using PWD from var-context to enable security validation. When run_bash_command(cmd, dir) executes, the shell sees PWD=dir, so "./file.txt" becomes "/dir/file.txt". The parser must extract this exact absolute path for scope.yml validation. Without this, relative paths would bypass security checks.

Design pattern:
Follow existing variable resolution pattern in jf/bash-resolve-variables:
- Check for context availability (PWD in var-context)
- Return original path unchanged if context missing (security: mark as unresolved)
- Use Emacs path manipulation functions (file-name-directory, expand-file-name)
- Integrate into existing file-ops extraction pipeline after variable resolution

Verification:
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-relative-path-'
- Expected: 11 tests should pass (currently marked :expected-result :failed)
- Tests cover: . alone, ./ prefix, ../ parent navigation, script execution, mixed paths
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-pwd-context-'  
- Expected: test-pwd-context-mixed-absolute-relative and test-pwd-context-multiple-operations should pass
- Run: make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-execution-parity-'
- Expected: test-execution-parity-relative-path should pass (validates parser matches execution)

Test files:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-pwd-directory-context.el (lines 101-253 have relative path tests)

Example behavior:
  (jf/bash-resolve-relative-path "." '((PWD . "/base/dir")))
    => "/base/dir"
  (jf/bash-resolve-relative-path "./file.txt" '((PWD . "/base/dir")))
    => "/base/dir/file.txt"
  (jf/bash-resolve-relative-path "../other/file.txt" '((PWD . "/base/dir/sub")))
    => "/base/dir/other/file.txt"
  (jf/bash-resolve-relative-path "./file.txt" nil)
    => "./file.txt" (unchanged - no PWD context)

title: scope-core bash validator should use scope-shell-tools helper functions
description:
The jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool in scope-core.el (lines 608-669) duplicates command parsing and categorization logic instead of calling helper functions from scope-shell-tools.el:
- jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command (line 51)
- jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category (line 74)

This violates DRY and creates maintenance risk. The helpers exist for reuse but aren't being used by the validator.

Impact: Code duplication, potential behavior divergence, harder maintenance.
close_reason:
Duplication already resolved by removing unused helpers (emacs-t01a)

The bash validation logic duplication was resolved by bead emacs-t01a, which removed the unused helper functions (jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command and jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category) from scope-shell-tools.

The validator in scope-core (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool) is now the single source of truth for bash validation logic. The helpers were never actually used - the gptel-make-scoped-tool macro calls the scope-core validator directly.

This approach (consolidate in scope-core, remove unused helpers) is cleaner than extracting helpers because it keeps all validation logic together in the scope system.

title: Implement jf/bash--glob-to-regex helper function
description:
## Problem

Five tests are failing because the function jf/bash--glob-to-regex doesn't exist:

## Expected Behavior

The function should convert bash glob patterns to Emacs regex patterns:
- * → [^/]* (match anything except /)

## Resolution

Implement jf/bash--glob-to-regex in config/experiments/bash-parser/glob-matching.el with proper conversions for:
1. Asterisk wildcard
2. Question mark wildcard
3. Character classes
4. Regex special character escaping
5. Literal parentheses handling

Tests already exist in test/test-glob-matching.el:221-260.

title: Allow-once resource format mismatch for bash commands
description:
Integration bug in allow-once mechanism for bash commands:

scope-core.el line 303 constructs bash allow-once resource as:
  format("%s:%s", command, expand-file-name(directory))
  Example: "tree:/Users/jefffarr/emacs"

But scope-expansion.el line 112 adds to allow-once list using:
  resource (from violation-info)
  Which comes from request_scope_expansion patterns: ["tree"]

Result: allow-once list contains "tree" but validator checks for "tree:/Users/jefffarr/emacs"

Impact: Allow-once approvals for bash commands will never match and commands will be denied even after user approves them temporarily.

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (line 303)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el (line 112)
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el (request_scope_expansion tool)
close_reason:
Fix allow-once resource format mismatch for bash commands

Changed allow-once resource construction for bash tools from 'command:directory' to just 'command' (line 573). This matches the format provided by request_scope_expansion, which passes command patterns like ['tree'] not ['tree:/path/to/dir'].

Before: ('bash (format "%s:%s" (car args) (expand-file-name (cadr args))))
After: ('bash (car args))

This ensures allow-once approvals for bash commands work correctly - when user approves 'tree' temporarily, the next 'tree' invocation will match and be allowed.

title: Add bash_tools section to research.yml template
description:
The research.yml profile has shell_commands configuration but is missing the bash_tools section entirely. Since it allows read-only commands like ls, find, grep, rg, it should have a bash_tools section with appropriate read_only category.

Recommended addition after shell_commands section:
bash_tools:
  categories:
    read_only: ["ls", "find", "grep", "rg", "pwd", "which"]
    safe_write: []
    dangerous: []
  deny:
    - "rm"
    - "sudo"
    - "mv"
    - "chmod"

Script execution:

Semantics:

- test-semantics-lookup-egrep-skip-rules

**Design context:**

Tests test-script-execution-go-{build,run,test} and test-semantics-{flag-dependent-tar,lookup-command-with-skip-rules,lookup-egrep-skip-rules} now pass.

title: Implement pattern matching, text properties, overlays, and visual feedback
description:
**Summary:** Real-time detection & visual feedback for command references

**Files to modify:**
- config/gptel/commands/commands-detection.org (create)
- config/gptel/commands/commands-detection.el (tangled output)

**Implementation steps:**

1. Create literate org file with standard headers

2. Define custom face:
   ```elisp
   (defface gptel-command-face
     '((t :foreground "light blue"))
     "Face for command references in gptel buffers."
     :group 'gptel-faces)
   ```

3. Implement pattern matching:
   - Regex: \\(?:^\\|[[:space:]]\\)\\(/\\([a-z0-9:-]+\\)\\(?:[[:space:]]+\\(.+?\\)\\)?\\)\\s-*$
   - Captures: full match, command name, optional args
   - Requires whitespace before / or start of line (avoids http:// false positives)

4. Implement command reference parsing, text property application, overlay creation

5. Implement tooltip generation from frontmatter

6. Implement detection on buffer change via after-change-functions

7. Hook registration via gptel-mode-hook

**Design rationale:**

Text properties persist, overlays provide presentation - separation of data and visual feedback. after-change-functions enables real-time detection. Pattern requires whitespace before / to prevent false positives. Property value (cons 'command ...) aligns with upstream gptel pattern.

**Verification:**

1. Type /opsx:explore - should see light blue styling and tooltip
2. Type /nonexistent - should NOT be styled
3. Type http://example.com/path - should NOT detect /path as command
4. Edit command reference - overlay should update correctly

**Context:** design.md § Decisions 2, 4; specs/gptel/commands.md § Visual feedback

title: Warn user when preset specifies model not in backend configuration
description:
## Problem

When a preset file specifies a model that isn't in the backend's :models list, gptel silently falls back to a default model (typically the first one in the list). This causes confusing behavior where users select a preset expecting one model but get another.

## Current Behavior

1. User creates preset with `model: claude-opus-4-6`
2. Backend only has `claude-sonnet-4-6` configured
3. User selects preset, expects opus
4. Session silently uses sonnet (or haiku) instead
5. No warning, error, or indication that model wasn't available

## Expected Behavior

System should notify the user when:
- Loading a preset that references an unconfigured model
- OR when applying preset settings to a session

Notification could be:
- Warning message in minibuffer
- Error with clear message about missing model
- Validation at preset load time with list of available models

## Discovery Context

Found during testing of gptel-preset-upstream-alignment where all preset files had invalid model names (e.g., `claude-opus-4-5-20251101` instead of `claude-opus-4-6`). Sessions silently fell back to haiku with no indication of the misconfiguration.

## Related Files

- `config/gptel/sessions/preset-registration.el` - Preset loading
- `config/gptel/presets/*.md` - Preset definitions
- `config/gptel/gptel.org` - Backend configuration with :models list

title: Command substitution semantic gap detection failing
description:
Three semantic gap detection tests are failing for command substitution patterns:

1. test-corpus-integration-001: Simple command substitution (echo $(whoami)) - Expected 'command-substitution', got nil
2. test-corpus-integration-002: Nested command substitution (ls -la $(dirname $(which openspec))) - Expected 'command-substitution', got nil  
3. test-corpus-integration-003: Command substitution in quoted string (--eval '(setq dir "'$(pwd)'")')  - Expected 'command-substitution', got nil

The parser is not detecting $(...) command substitutions as semantic gaps. Process substitution detection (<(...)) works correctly, but command substitution patterns are not being identified.

Files to investigate:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.el (semantic gap detection)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (AST node handling)

Implementation steps:
1. Check if bash-parser-semantics.el has handlers for command substitution nodes
2. Verify that bash-parser-core.el properly identifies command_substitution nodes in the AST
3. Add or fix the semantic gap detection for $(...) patterns
4. Ensure nested command substitutions are detected recursively

Design rationale: Command substitutions are semantic gaps because they execute shell commands whose output affects the command being analyzed. Without detecting these gaps, the parser cannot accurately determine file operations that depend on command output.

Verification:
- Run make test-bash-parser
- Confirm test-corpus-integration-001, 002, 003 pass
- Verify nested command substitutions are detected properly

title: Update gptel.org loader: drop gptel-agent, add preset registration
description:
Update gptel.org loader: drop gptel-agent, add preset registration

## Implementation steps

   - Place BEFORE session module loading (constants, commands, etc.)
4. Add `(jf/gptel-preset-register-all)` call immediately after module load
5. Update skills `@mention` expansion function `jf/gptel-agent--expand-all-agent-skills` (lines ~207-258):
   - Change `(boundp 'gptel-agent--agents)` guard to `(bound-and-true-p gptel--known-presets)`
   - Change `(dolist (agent-entry gptel-agent--agents) ...)` to iterate `gptel--known-presets`
   - Each entry in `gptel--known-presets` is `(name . plist)` — extract `:system` from plist for skill expansion
   - Update the expanded system message back into the registered preset
6. Remove any advice attachment to `gptel-agent-update` (if present in skills expansion setup)
7. Ensure final load order in `:config` block: yaml.el → preset-registration module → register-all → skills expansion → session hooks
8. Tangle and validate with `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/gptel.org`

## Design rationale

gptel-agent's only functional dependency was the YAML frontmatter parser (`gptel-agent-read-file`), now replaced by our own registration module. The "Agent" tool is replaced by PersistentAgent. The two hardcoded `gptel-make-preset` calls in `gptel-agent-update` are superseded by the registration pipeline. Presets must register before session hooks fire so upstream's `gptel--restore-state` can find presets by name.

No global default tools — presets define their own. Non-session `M-x gptel` buffers start toolless (upstream's default behavior).

## Verification

- `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/gptel.org` passes
- Emacs starts without gptel-agent loaded (`(featurep 'gptel-agent)` → nil)
- `gptel-agent--agents` is not bound
- Presets appear in gptel transient menu
- Skills `@mention` expansion runs without error against `gptel--known-presets`

Context: design.md § Decisions 8-9, gptel/core.md

title: Verify scope-core loads and normalizes bash_tools config
description:
The jf/gptel-scope--load-config function in scope-core.org must correctly load and normalize bash_tools from scope.yml.

Check lines 256-310 in scope-core.org:
1. Does jf/gptel-scope--parse-scope-yml extract bash_tools from YAML?
2. Does it normalize keys from bash_tools (underscore) to bash-tools (hyphen)?
3. Does it preserve the nested structure with categories?

Expected structure after parsing:
  :bash-tools (
    :categories (
      :read-only (:commands ["ls" "grep" ...])
      :safe-write (:commands ["mkdir" ...])
      :dangerous (:commands [...])
    )
    :deny ["rm" "mv" ...]
  )

The validator expects this structure when accessing (plist-get bash-config :categories).
notes: VERIFIED: scope-core.el lines 325-328 parse YAML and call jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys, converting bash_tools→bash-tools, read_only→read-only at all nesting levels.

title: Module dependency cycle risk: scope-expansion requires scope-core but writes bash_tools
description:
## Observation

Current dependency graph:
```
scope-core.el (validator, loaded first)
  ↓
scope-expansion.el (UI, requires scope-core)
  ↓ (calls jf/gptel-scope--add-to-allow-once-list from scope-core)
  ↓ (accesses jf/gptel-scope--tool-categories from scope-core)
  
scope-shell-tools.el (tools, requires scope-core + scope-expansion)
  ↓ (uses gptel-make-scoped-tool macro from scope-core)
  ↓ (calls jf/gptel-scope--prompt-expansion from scope-expansion)
```

## Dependency Flow

Load order (gptel.org):
1. scope-core.el (line 240) - Base validator
2. scope-expansion.el (line 307) - UI layer
3. scope-shell-tools.el (line 310) - Tool implementations

## Potential Risk

scope-expansion.el writes bash_tools YAML (line 257-308), which contains bash-specific logic:
- Determines category from tool operation (read vs write)
- Knows about :read-only and :safe-write categories
- Writes nested bash_tools.categories structure

This creates knowledge coupling: scope-expansion knows about bash_tools schema.

## Current State

✓ No circular dependency (load order is linear)
✓ All dependencies flow downward
✗ Knowledge coupling: expansion layer knows bash schema details

## Recommendation

Consider extracting bash_tools YAML writing to scope-shell-tools.el:
```elisp
;; In scope-expansion.el
('bash
 (jf/gptel-bash--add-to-scope scope-file resource tool))

;; Define jf/gptel-bash--add-to-scope in scope-shell-tools.el
```

This would centralize bash-specific knowledge in the bash module.

## Status

Low priority - current design works, but violates separation of concerns slightly.

## Files
- config/gptel/gptel.org (load order)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el (jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope)
close_reason: Verified: No circular dependency exists. Knowledge coupling (expansion knows bash schema) is acceptable - centralized YAML writing pattern. Alternative of moving bash YAML logic to shell-tools would spread YAML handling across modules. Current design is intentional. Documented in MODULE_DEPENDENCIES.md

title: CRITICAL: scope-shell-tools categorize command accesses wrong config structure
description:
## Issue

scope-shell-tools.el lines 51-64:

```elisp
(defun jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command (command config)
  (let* ((categories (plist-get config :categories))
         (deny-list (plist-get config :deny))
         (read-only (plist-get (plist-get categories :read-only) :commands))
         (safe-write (plist-get (plist-get categories :safe-write) :commands))
         (dangerous (plist-get (plist-get categories :dangerous) :commands)))
```

This function receives CONFIG as the bash-config plist directly, but tries to get :categories from it.

However, scope-core.el line 562 calls it like:
```elisp
(bash-config (plist-get config :bash-tools))
(categories (plist-get bash-config :categories))
```

So bash-config already HAS :categories as a key, but the categorize function is passed CONFIG (the full scope config), not bash-config.

## Root Cause

scope-shell-tools.el line 51 expects to receive the bash_tools section, but scope-core.el doesn't pass bash-config directly to this helper.

Wait - let me re-check. Looking at scope-shell-tools.el, jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command is only called WITHIN that file, not from scope-core.el.

Let me trace the actual usage...

Actually, looking at the file, jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command is NOT used anywhere in the current implementation! The categorization logic is DUPLICATED in scope-core.el lines 584-601.

## Actual Issue

**Dead Code**: jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command in scope-shell-tools.el is never called.

**Code Duplication**: Categorization logic exists in both:
1. scope-shell-tools.el lines 51-64 (unused)
2. scope-core.el lines 584-601 (active)

## Recommendation

Either:
1. Remove jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command from scope-shell-tools.el (dead code)
2. OR refactor scope-core.el to call it (DRY principle)

## Files
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el (jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool)

coding.yml denies: rm, sudo

title: Update OpenSpec artifacts: remove outdated branch refs
description:

Multiple OpenSpec artifacts reference gptel-preset-alignment as if it's still on a separate branch, but it has been merged into both main and the current branch (gptel-scoped-bash-tools).

title: Fix missing require for jf/gptel--scope-file-path in scope-expansion
description:
## Context
Comprehensive code review found a missing module dependency that will cause runtime errors.

## Issue
`config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el` calls `jf/gptel--scope-file-path` at lines 66 and 133, but this function is defined in `config/gptel/sessions/filesystem.el`. The scope-expansion module only requires `jf-gptel-scope-core` and `yaml` — there is no `(require 'gptel-session-filesystem)`.

This will cause a void-function error at runtime if filesystem.el hasn't been loaded through some other path before scope-expansion is invoked.

## Fix
Either:
- Add `(require 'gptel-session-filesystem)` to scope-expansion.el, OR
- Replace the calls with `(expand-file-name jf/gptel-session--scope-file dir)` which does the same thing without needing the function (the constant is already available via scope-core's require of gptel-session-constants)

## Files
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.org (requires section + lines ~66, ~133)

## Discovered from
Comprehensive review of emacs-cff

comments.author: Jeff Farr

title: [MEDIUM] PersistentAgent: Overlay validity not checked before deletion
description:
## Problem

Overlay deletion calls don't check if overlay buffer still exists, causing failures when parent buffer is killed during execution.

## Spec Requirement

**openspec/specs/gptel/persistent-agent.md lines 290-295:**
> Overlay validity checked before updates
> - WHEN FSM handler tries to update overlay
> - THEN the system checks (overlay-buffer overlay) returns non-nil
> - AND skips update if parent buffer was killed
> - AND prevents crashes from dangling overlay references

**Lines 404-408:**
> Overlay always cleaned up
> - WHEN ANY terminal state is reached
> - THEN the callback MUST call (delete-overlay ov)
> - AND no dangling overlays persist after completion

## Current Implementation

**config/gptel/tools/persistent-agent.org:**
- Line 725: `(delete-overlay ov)` - no validity check (network error path)
- Line 732: `(delete-overlay ov)` - no validity check (user abort path)  
- Line 766: `(delete-overlay ov)` - no validity check (success path)

FSM handlers DO check validity (lines 505, 523), but deletion sites don't.

## Impact

- Could fail/error if parent buffer killed during agent execution
- Inconsistent with overlay update pattern (which checks validity)

## Fix

Wrap all delete-overlay calls with validity check:
```elisp
(when (overlay-buffer ov)
  (delete-overlay ov))
```

Apply to lines 725, 732, 766.

## Verification

Agent ID: a346c94

title: Pattern not detected in test expressions
description:
Tests: test-corpus-integration-001, test-corpus-integration-003

Issue: When glob patterns appear in test expressions like [ -f "*.log" ], the pattern is not being properly detected and quotes are being included in file paths.

test-corpus-integration-001:
Expected: (:file "*.log" :operation :read-metadata :test-condition t :loop-context t :test-operator "-f" :pattern t)
Actual: (:file "\"*.log\"" :operation :read-metadata ...) -- quotes included, pattern detection may be wrong

test-corpus-integration-003:
Expected: (:file "*/" :operation :read-metadata :test-condition t :loop-context t :test-operator "-d" :pattern t)  
Actual: (:file "\"*//config\"" :operation :read-metadata ...) -- extra path component, pattern not recognized correctly

Files to investigate:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (test expression extraction)
- Look for jf/bash--extract-test-expression-operations
- Check jf/bash--has-glob-pattern-p usage

Implementation:
1. Find test expression extractor function
2. Strip quotes from file paths before checking for glob patterns
3. Ensure jf/bash--has-glob-pattern-p is called on unquoted paths
4. Verify :pattern t metadata is set when glob patterns detected
5. Handle variable expansion in test expressions (e.g., "$dir/config")

Design rationale: Test expressions often use quoted glob patterns for file existence checks in loops. Proper pattern detection is critical for understanding which files are actually being tested vs. which are glob patterns that expand at runtime.

Verification:
- Run make test-bash-parser
- Confirm test-corpus-integration-001 and 003 pass
- Test various patterns: *.log, */, **/*.txt
- Verify quotes are stripped but patterns detected

title: Nested backtick command substitutions not fully extracted
description:
Test: test-cmdsub-nested-backticks
Command: echo \`echo \\\`date\\\`\`

**Current behavior:**
Parser extracts only the outer backtick command substitution, not the nested inner one.

Actual: 1 substitution found
- [0] syntax=\` content="echo \\\`date\\\`" nesting=1

Expected: 2 substitutions found
- [0] syntax=\` content="echo \\\\\\\`date\\\\\\\`" nesting=1 (outer)
- [1] syntax=\` content="date" nesting=2 (inner)

**Root cause:**
Command substitution extraction logic doesn't recursively extract nested backticks from within already-found substitutions. The parser finds the outer backtick but doesn't descend into it to find the inner \`date\`.

This contrasts with $() syntax which may handle nesting correctly.

**Files to investigate:**
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-core.el (jf/bash-parse--extract-command-substitutions)
- Look for recursive extraction logic
- Check if $() nesting works but backtick nesting doesn't
- May need to recursively parse the :content of found substitutions

**Verification:**
```bash
make test-pattern PATTERN='^test-cmdsub-nested-backticks$'
```

**Note:**
Backtick nesting requires escaping: outer backticks contain escaped inner backticks (\\\`).
close_reason: Fixed nested backtick command substitution extraction. Added helper functions to unescape and recursively extract nested backticks within command substitutions. Test test-cmdsub-nested-backticks now passes.

title: [CRITICAL] PersistentAgent: Wrong tool category - should be 'meta' not 'gptel-persistent'
description:
## Problem

PersistentAgent tool is registered with wrong category, preventing it from bypassing scope validation as specified.

## Spec Requirement

**openspec/specs/gptel/persistent-agent.md lines 20, 39-42:**
- Tool SHALL be categorized as 'meta' in the scope system
- Meta tools bypass scope validation for tool invocation itself
- Agent's own tools still respect agent's scope

## Current Implementation

**config/gptel/tools/persistent-agent.org line 823:**
```elisp
:category "gptel-persistent"
```

## Impact

- PersistentAgent may be subject to scope validation checks when invoked by parent sessions
- Could prevent agents from spawning in sessions with restrictive scope configurations
- Contradicts explicit spec requirement for scope bypass

## Fix

Change line 823 from:
```elisp
:category "gptel-persistent"
```

To:
```elisp
:category "meta"
```

## Verification

Agent ID: a3a5442

title: Clean up dead template references and gptel-agent remnants in scope-commands
description:
## Context
Code review of emacs-mhk found that scope template functions were deleted but their call site was not updated, and gptel-agent references remain in functional code.

## Issues

- Dynamically calls jf/gptel-scope--template-deny-all, template-codebase-read, etc. via (intern (format ...))

Fix: Either remove jf/gptel-scope-edit-plan entirely (replaced by scope profiles) or update it to use scope profile YAML files instead.

### Issue 2: gptel-agent still referenced in scope-commands
- scope-commands.el:98: (require gptel-agent nil t)
- scope-commands.el:99-100: (when (fboundp gptel-agent-read-file) ...) in jf/gptel-scope--parse-preset-tools
- The fboundp guard prevents errors but preset tools parsing silently returns nil
- This was the bead verification step 7: "grep -r gptel-agent config/gptel/ returns NO hits" - this fails

Fix: Update jf/gptel-scope--parse-preset-tools to read tools from gptel--known-presets instead of gptel-agent-read-file. Remove the (require gptel-agent nil t).

title: Update session creation and save to use scope.yml/metadata.yml
description:
Update session creation and save to use scope.yml/metadata.yml

## Files to modify
- `config/gptel/sessions/commands.org`

## Implementation steps

1. Update `jf/gptel--create-session-core`:
   - Write `scope.yml` from preset's scope profile via `jf/gptel-scope-profile--resolve` + `jf/gptel-scope-profile--write-scope-yml`
   - Write `metadata.yml` with fields: `session_id`, `created` (ISO8601), `updated` (ISO8601), `preset` (preset name string)
   - NO longer call `jf/gptel--copy-preset-template` — do not copy `preset.md` to session directory
   - Delegate preset application to find-file-hook: write metadata.yml (with preset name) and scope.yml BEFORE opening session.md
   - When `worktree-paths` are provided (activities integration): write explicit paths to scope.yml bypassing profile template expansion
2. Update `jf/gptel-persistent-session`:
   - Select presets from `gptel--known-presets` (symbol names) instead of filesystem scanning via `jf/gptel--list-preset-templates`
   - Show preset `:description` as completion annotation
   - Pass preset name symbol to `jf/gptel--create-session-core`
3. Remove functions that are no longer needed:
   - `jf/gptel--load-preset-from-file` — replaced by `gptel--apply-preset` in open hook
   - `jf/gptel--apply-session-preset` — replaced by `gptel--apply-preset` in open hook
   - `jf/gptel--normalize-preset-for-serialization` — no longer needed
   - `jf/gptel--write-preset-md`, `jf/gptel--write-preset-org`, `jf/gptel--write-preset-file` — no preset.md in sessions
   - `jf/gptel--list-preset-templates`, `jf/gptel--resolve-preset-template`, `jf/gptel--select-preset-template` — replaced by `gptel--known-presets` iteration
   - `jf/gptel--copy-preset-template` — no longer copies preset files
4. Remove `jf/gptel--skip-system-message-save-advice`:
   - Remove the advice function definition
   - Remove the `advice-add` on `gptel--save-state`
   - Upstream's `gptel--preset-mismatch-value` automatically omits system message when it matches the registered preset
5. Remove `jf/gptel--set-session-system-message` if it only existed to work around save-prevention
6. Tangle and validate

## Design rationale

Session creation writes scope.yml + metadata.yml, then opens session.md. The find-file-hook's "new session" path reads the preset name from metadata.yml and applies it — this avoids duplicating preset application logic between creation and open. Upstream's differential save handles save semantics: it writes `gptel--preset` plus only values that differ from the preset. System message save-prevention advice is unnecessary because `gptel--preset-mismatch-value` already handles this.

## Verification

- `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/sessions/commands.org` passes
- `jf/gptel-persistent-session` shows registered presets in completion
- New session creates directory with: `session.md`, `scope.yml`, `metadata.yml` (NO `preset.md`, NO `scope-plan.yml`)
- Preset applied via find-file-hook after session.md opens
- Save writes only `gptel--preset` + overridden values to Local Variables
- System message NOT written if it matches preset (verified by inspecting Local Variables block)

Context: design.md § Decisions 5-6, Migration Phase 2, sessions-persistence.md § Create & Save lifecycle

title: Document command substitution operation semantics and test patterns
description:
Document the semantics of command substitution operations to prevent future test expectation mismatches.

**CONTEXT:**

The test-recursive-single-substitution regression revealed ambiguity about what operations should be created for commands with substitutions:

Command: cat $(find . -name config.yml)

Three different operation models possible:
1. Create operation with :file = "$(find . -name config.yml)" (original behavior)
2. Skip substitution args entirely, rely on pattern flow (Agent 2's change)
3. Create special substitution-consumption operation (not implemented)

This caused confusion between what the test expected vs what the implementation provides.

**CURRENT BEHAVIOR (After Agent 2 - emacs-vcrm):**

For: cat $(find . -name config.yml)

Operations created:
1. (:file "." :operation :read-directory :from-substitution t :command "find")
2. (:file "config.yml" :operation :match-pattern :from-substitution t :command "find")  
3. (:file "config.yml" :operation :read :command "cat" :pattern-source ...)

Key insight: Operation #3 captures the relationship through :pattern-source metadata, NOT through the :file field containing the substitution string.

**DOCUMENTATION NEEDED:**

Files to create/modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/README.md (or create ARCHITECTURE.md)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-bash-parser-recursive.el (add comments)

Documentation should cover:

1. **Operation Creation Rules for Substitutions:**
   - Arguments starting with $( are NOT treated as literal file paths
   - Substitution content is parsed recursively (operations marked :from-substitution t)
   - Outer command operations reference the RESULT (pattern), not the SYNTAX ($(...)
   - Pattern flow tracking links producer to consumer via :pattern-source

2. **Test Patterns:**
   - How to test that substitution was processed: Check for :from-substitution operations
   - How to test outer command consumption: Check for pattern operation with :pattern-source
   - Anti-pattern: Checking for substitution syntax in :file field
   - Example test assertions for common scenarios

3. **Metadata Fields:**
   - :from-substitution - marks operations from recursive substitution parsing
   - :pattern-source - links consumer operation to pattern producer
   - :pattern - indicates file path contains glob pattern
   - When each field should be present

4. **Design Rationale:**
   - Why we don't create operations for substitution syntax strings
   - How pattern flow operations capture semantic relationships
   - Tradeoffs between explicit vs implicit tracking

Implementation:
1. Create ARCHITECTURE.md in config/experiments/bash-parser/ documenting operation semantics
2. Add detailed comments to test-bash-parser-recursive.el explaining test patterns
3. Include examples of correct vs incorrect test assertions
4. Document the evolution: before Agent 2's change vs after

Verification:
- Documentation should be clear enough to prevent future regressions
- Test patterns should be reusable for new substitution tests
- Rationale should explain why the current approach is correct

Design rationale:
Good documentation prevents future implementers from making assumptions that conflict with actual semantics. The regression happened because test expectations didn't match implementation reality. Clear documentation of both the 'what' and 'why' prevents this.

Related beads:
- emacs-6dpn (the regression fix that prompted this documentation need)

title: Extraction from Redirections
description:
Extract file operations from redirection operators with high confidence.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "File Operations Extraction - Redirections"
2. Implement `jf/bash-extract-operations-from-redirections` function
3. Handle all redirection types from parsed `:redirections` field:
   - ">" → :write operation
   - ">>" → :append operation
   - "<" → :read operation
   - "2>" → :write operation (stderr)
   - "&>" → :write operation (stdout+stderr)
4. Extract target path from redirection
5. Return operation plists with:
   - :file (target path)
   - :operation (:read, :write, :append)
   - :confidence :high (redirections are unambiguous)
   - :source :redirection
   - :metadata (original redirection data)

Design rationale:
Redirections are grammar-level constructs, always high confidence. The parser already extracts them into :redirections field, we just map operators to operation types. No semantic ambiguity - ">" always writes, "<" always reads. This is the most reliable source of file operations.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-extract-operations-from-redirections (parsed-command)
  "Extract file operations from :redirections field with high confidence."
  (let ((redirections (plist-get parsed-command :redirections))
        (operations nil))
    (dolist (redir redirections)
      (let* ((type (plist-get redir :type))
             (target (plist-get redir :target))
             (operation (pcase type
                         (:output :write)
                         (:append :append)
                         (:input :read)
                         (:error :write)
                         (:both :write))))
        (push (list :file target
                    :operation operation
                    :confidence :high
                    :source :redirection
                    :metadata redir)
              operations)))
    (nreverse operations)))
```

Verification:
- "echo test > output.txt" extracts (:file "output.txt" :operation :write :confidence :high)
- "cat < input.txt" extracts (:file "input.txt" :operation :read :confidence :high)
- "cmd >> log.txt" extracts (:file "log.txt" :operation :append :confidence :high)
- "cmd 2> err.txt" extracts (:file "err.txt" :operation :write :confidence :high)
- Test with all redirection scenarios from bash-file-operations/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 2: File Operation Extraction Architecture"

title: Fix script-args extraction for interpreter commands
description:
Fix script-args extraction to properly capture arguments passed to executed scripts, including both file arguments and flag arguments.

CONTEXT:
8 script execution tests are failing because :script-args field is not being populated correctly. The tests expect script arguments to be captured for ALL execute operations, not just index 0 operations.

Current behavior: bash-parser-file-ops.el line 384-386 only captures script-args when index=0
Expected behavior: Capture script-args for ALL execute operations

Failing tests:
- test-script-execution-node-with-flags (expects --port, 3000, --verbose)
- test-script-execution-script-args-flags
- test-script-execution-script-args-files
- test-script-execution-corpus (multiple cases)
- test-script-execution-go-run-subcommand
- test-script-execution-go-test-subcommand
- test-script-execution-go-build-not-execute
- test-script-execution-nested-python

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (tangled output)

Implementation steps:

1. Fix script-args extraction in jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs
   Current code at lines 382-386 only captures when index=0
   
   Change to capture script-args for ANY execute operation:
   - For positional arg at index N with :execute operation
   - Script-args are elements from index N+1 onward
   - Use seq-subseq to extract remaining args

2. Ensure script-args handling for self-executing scripts
   In jf/bash--extract-from-single-command (line 234-240):
   Already correctly captures positional-args as :script-args - no change needed

3. Handle script-args for nested commands (bash -c, sh -c)
   These should also preserve script arguments when extracting from nested command string

Design rationale:
When interpreters execute scripts, everything after the script path becomes arguments TO the script, not to the interpreter. For example:
- python script.py arg1 arg2 → script-args: (arg1 arg2)
- node server.js --port 3000 → script-args: (--port 3000)
- go run main.go --config prod.yaml → script-args: (--config prod.yaml)

This information is critical for security analysis - script arguments may contain file paths, credentials, or other sensitive data.

Test verification:
Run: ./bin/run-tests.sh -d config/experiments/bash-parser/test -p test-script-execution-
Expected: All script-execution tests should pass, with proper :script-args populated

Acceptance criteria:
- test-script-execution-node-with-flags passes
- test-script-execution-script-args-* tests pass
- test-script-execution-corpus passes all 40+ corpus cases
- :script-args field contains empty list when no args, not nil
- :script-args field present for ALL :execute operations

title: Transient scope callback handling could fail silently
description:
In scope-expansion.org, the transient suffix functions call callbacks but error handling is incomplete:

jf/gptel-scope--deny-expansion (line 78):
  Line 82-83: (when callback (funcall callback ...))
  
jf/gptel-scope--add-to-scope (line 95):
  Line 132-140: (when callback (funcall callback ...))
  
jf/gptel-scope--allow-once-action (line 149):
  Line 162-167: (when callback (funcall callback ...))

Issues:

1. No error handling around funcall
   - If callback errors, user sees cryptic error in transient UI
   - Error could be from json-serialize (line 85, 136, 163) or from callback itself
   - User cannot distinguish between 'callback failed' vs 'JSON serialization failed' vs 'transient error'

2. (when callback ...) silently skips if callback is nil
   - This might be intentional (allow testing without callback)
   - But if callback is unexpectedly nil, no warning given
   - User approves expansion but nothing happens
   
3. No validation that callback is a function
   - If callback is malformed (not a function), funcall will error
   - Should check (functionp callback) before calling

4. The json-serialize call could fail
   - If result plist has non-serializable values
   - If result structure doesn't match expected format
   - Error would be confusing: 'json error' in scope expansion context

5. After callback errors, transient-quit-one is not called
   - User stuck in transient menu
   - Must manually quit

Recommendation:
1. Wrap funcall in condition-case
2. Warn if callback is nil (unless this is intentional)
3. Always call transient-quit-one even on error
4. Show user-friendly error message on callback failure
close_reason: Add error handling to transient callbacks with condition-case wrappers

title: Capture script arguments metadata
description:
Extract and attach script arguments to execute operations for future analysis.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (literate source)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (tangled output)

Implementation steps:
1. Modify positional args extraction logic in bash-parser-file-ops.org:
   - When extracting execute operation with :index 0
   - Capture remaining positional args (index 1+) in :script-args field
   - Return empty list () if no arguments after script file
2. For interpreter commands (from semantics database):
   - Get all positional args from parsed command
   - Extract first arg (index 0) as :file
   - Capture remaining args as :script-args
   - Add :script-args to operation plist
3. For self-executing commands:
   - Get all positional args from parsed command
   - Add :script-args to self-execution operation plist
4. Ensure :script-args is always present (empty list if no args)
5. Tangle using ./bin/tangle-org.sh config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org
6. Verify syntax with check-parens

Design rationale:
- Preserves information for future analysis without creating false operations (Decision 3)
- Script arguments are unknown data types (files, URLs, flags, etc.)
- Metadata approach keeps operations list clean and focused
- Enables future features (annotation files, static analysis) without re-parsing
- Empty list () convention is clear and consistent

Design pattern:
Interpreter execution with script args:
(let* ((positional-args (plist-get parsed :positional-args))
       (script-file (car positional-args))
       (script-args (cdr positional-args)))
  (list :file script-file
        :operation :execute
        :source :positional-arg
        :confidence :high
        :script-args script-args))

Self-execution with script args:
(when (jf/bash--command-executes-self-p command-name)
  (list :file command-name
        :operation :execute
        :source :command-name
        :confidence :low
        :self-executing t
        :script-args (plist-get parsed :positional-args)))

Verification:
- python script.py includes :script-args ()
- python process.py input.csv output.json includes :script-args ("input.csv" "output.json")
- node server.js --port 3000 includes :script-args ("--port" "3000")
- ./deploy.sh staging us-west-2 includes :script-args ("staging" "us-west-2")
- All execute operations have :script-args field (never missing)
- Existing tests continue to pass
- Tangle validates without errors

Context: design.md § Decision 3 (Script arguments metadata)

title: Script execution corpus tests regressed (4 cases)
description:
Test: test-script-execution-corpus

Four test cases failing after flag-dependent interpreter changes (commit 8451f62 claimed to fix these):

1. exec-edge-002: Python -c with inline code
   - Expected: 0 operations
   - Actual: 1 operation
   - Command: python -c 'print(1)'
   - Issue: -c flag not preventing file operation extraction

2. exec-nested-001: Nested python execution  
   - Expected: 1 operation
   - Actual: 2 operations
   - Command: bash -c 'python script.py'
   - Issue: Double-counting - both bash and python producing operations

3. exec-nested-003: Nested execution with arguments
   - Expected: 1 operation  
   - Actual: 2 operations
   - Command: bash -c 'python script.py arg1 arg2'
   - Issue: Same double-counting problem

4. exec-variable-003: Self-executing with variable (unresolved)
   - Expected: :unresolved t
   - Actual: :unresolved nil
   - Command: ./$SCRIPT_NAME input.txt
   - Issue: Variable not being flagged as unresolved

Files to investigate:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.org (interpreter flag handlers)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (flag matching logic, variable resolution)

Implementation:
1. Check flag-dependent handlers for python/python3/node/bash/sh/zsh
2. Verify -c/-m/-e/-p/--eval flags return empty operations list
3. Fix flag matching - may not be detecting flags in all contexts
4. For nested execution: ensure only innermost command extracts operations
5. Fix variable resolution to mark unresolved variables with :unresolved t

Design rationale: This is a P1 regression. Agent claimed these were fixed but they've broken again. The flag-dependent semantics changes may have been lost during .org file reconstruction or are not working correctly in nested/substitution contexts.

Verification:
- Run make test-bash-parser
- Confirm all 4 test cases pass
- Verify python -c produces 0 operations
- Verify nested execution produces 1 operation (not 2)
- Verify ./$VAR has :unresolved t

title: Fix head command: skip numeric argument after -n flag
description:
## Problem

One test is failing because head treats the numeric argument after -n as a file operation:

**test-corpus-read-003**: head -n 10 /tmp/log.txt
- Expected: 1 operation (:file "/tmp/log.txt" :operation :read)
- Actual: 2 operations 
  - (:file "10" :operation :read)  ← WRONG!
  - (:file "/tmp/log.txt" :operation :read)

## Root Cause

The head command semantics don't have a skip rule for the -n flag's argument. The number '10' is being treated as a file path.

## Expected Behavior

head command flags that take arguments should skip them:
- -n NUM (number of lines)
- -c NUM (number of bytes)  
- -q (quiet, no arg)
- -v (verbose, no arg)

Only positional arguments after flags should be treated as files.

## Resolution

Update head command semantics in config/experiments/bash-parser/command-semantics.el:

1. Add skip rule for -n flag (argument is line count, not a file)
2. Add skip rule for -c flag (argument is byte count, not a file)
3. Verify tail command has similar rules (tail -n, tail -c, tail -f)
4. Test with: head -n 10 file.txt, head -c 100 file.txt

Similar issue likely affects tail command - check and fix both together.

title: Error message format inconsistency in bash validator
description:
The bash validator in scope-core.org (lines 636-735) returns inconsistent error structures compared to other validators.

Issue 1: Missing :tool key in some error returns
- Line 638-641: Returns :tool but line 654 also returns :tool correctly
- Line 670-675: Returns :tool correctly
- BUT all paths should be consistent

Issue 2: Inconsistent use of :command vs :resource
- Bash validator uses :command, :directory fields (lines 618-623)
- Other validators use :resource field
- LLM sees different field names for different validation types

Issue 3: Custom :message field not used by format-tool-error
- Bash validator adds custom :message (lines 641, 654, 674, 684, 701, 717, 731)
- But jf/gptel-scope--format-tool-error (line 835-853) expects :message in check-result and uses it correctly
- This is actually CORRECT, but should be documented

Recommendation: Standardize error structure across all validators to use consistent field names.
close_reason: Document custom :message field usage in bash validator

title: Integration gap: scope-shell-tools helper functions not used by scope-core validator
description:
## Issue

scope-shell-tools.el defines several helper functions:
1. jf/gptel-bash--parse-command (line 31)
2. jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command (line 51)
3. jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category (line 73)
4. jf/gptel-bash--check-absolute-paths (line 129)
5. jf/gptel-bash--execute-command (line 142)

However, scope-core.el's jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool (lines 544-661) does NOT use ANY of these helpers. It:
- Parses command inline (line 570)
- Categorizes inline (lines 584-601)
- Validates directory inline (lines 630-658)

## Code Duplication

The validation logic is DUPLICATED between:
- scope-shell-tools.el (helper functions, unused)
- scope-core.el (inline in validator, active)

## Root Cause

Architectural separation issue:
- scope-core.el is the validator layer (should be pure validation)
- scope-shell-tools.el is the tool implementation layer (should handle bash execution)

BUT: The validator needs bash-specific logic (command parsing, categorization), so it was implemented inline rather than calling helpers.

## Impact

1. **Maintainability**: Changes to categorization logic must be made in TWO places
2. **Testing**: Helper functions in scope-shell-tools.el are not covered by actual code paths
3. **Coupling**: scope-core.el has bash-specific knowledge it shouldn't need

## Recommendation

Refactor to separate concerns:
1. Move bash parsing/categorization helpers to scope-core.el (they're validation logic)
2. Remove duplicate helpers from scope-shell-tools.el
3. Keep only execution helpers (jf/gptel-bash--execute-command) in scope-shell-tools.el

OR:

1. Make scope-core.el call scope-shell-tools.el helpers
2. Require scope-shell-tools.el in scope-core.el
3. Accept the dependency (validator depends on tool layer)

## Files
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el

title: Fix module name reference in design.md: scope-bash-tools → scope-shell-tools
description:

Line 199: - `config/gptel/scope/scope-bash-tools.{org,el}` - Replace `run_approved_command` with `run_bash_command`, add command parsing and categorization
Line 202: **New functions in scope-bash-tools.el:**
Line 440: - Relocate `scope-bash-tools.{org,el}` from `config/gptel/scope/` to `config/gptel/tools/`
Line 444: - Tangle and validate scope-bash-tools.org

The actual module is named scope-shell-tools (not scope-bash-tools) because it may expand to other shell commands in the future.

title: Review deny lists for completeness - missing dangerous commands
description:
Review the deny lists in both presets to ensure all truly dangerous commands are included.

Currently denied (bash-tools-example.md lines 67-74):
rm, mv, cp, chmod, chown, sudo, dd, mkfs

Currently denied (system-explorer.md lines 85-95):
rm, mv, cp, chmod, chown, sudo, dd, mkfs, kill, pkill

Potentially missing dangerous commands:
- rmdir: Remove directories (similar risk to rm)
- ln: Create links (can overwrite, create symlinks to sensitive files)
- curl/wget with -o: Download and write files
- scp/rsync: Remote file operations with write capability
- tar/zip with -x: Extract archives (can overwrite files)
- useradd/userdel: User management (system modification)
- systemctl/service: System service control
- reboot/shutdown: System power operations
- iptables/pfctl: Firewall modification
- crontab: Schedule automated tasks

Considerations:
1. Some commands (curl, tar) are useful for read operations but dangerous for write
2. The current model doesn't support 'command + flag' validation (only base command)
3. May need to be conservative and deny commands with any dangerous subcommands

Recommendation: Expand deny lists or document known risks for commands with mixed safety profiles.

- /Users/jefffarr/emacs/config/gptel/presets/system-explorer.md (lines 85-95)

P2 beads (high priority):
- emacs-4ma1: rmdir (remove directories)
- emacs-m6gr: ln (create links, can overwrite/symlink)
- emacs-zv1z: scp/rsync (remote file operations)

title: Update bash parser test corpus for :args field
description:
The addition of the :args field to preserve original argument order (including flags) requires updating test corpus expectations.

**Impact**: 32 tests currently failing because expected output doesn't include :args field:
- Parse corpus tests (chain, pipeline, complex commands)
- Command substitution tests

**Required Changes**:
- Add :args field to all expected command structures in test-corpus-parse.el
- Add :args field to command substitution test expectations
- Add :args field to file operations variable chain tests
- Update find exec-block expectations

**Approach**:
1. For each expected command structure, add :args field containing flags + positional-args in original order
2. For commands with no args, :args should be ()
3. For commands with only positional args, :args = positional-args
4. For commands with flags and positional args, :args = flags + positional-args

**Files to Update**:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-parse.el (main parse corpus)

title: scope-core.org bash_tools schema uses incorrect key format in documentation
description:
The scope-core.org documentation shows bash_tools schema with underscored nested keys (line 117: bash_tools), but the actual parsing code normalizes ALL keys to kebab-case, including nested structures.

Documentation (lines 92-106):


Issue:
- Line 117 says 'bash_tools.categories' - implies underscore format
- But jf/gptel-scope--normalize-plist-keys (lines 288-308) recursively normalizes ALL keys
- Result in elisp: :bash-tools -> :categories -> :read-only (all kebab-case)
- YAML on disk correctly uses snake_case (bash_tools, read_only)

The confusion:
- Documentation comment at line 117 should clarify that YAML uses snake_case but elisp uses kebab-case
- Or example should show the elisp representation: :bash-tools :categories :read-only

Files affected:
- config/gptel/scope-core.org (lines 112-119, 266)

Impact: LOW - Code works correctly, but documentation could confuse developers
close_reason: Fixed: Added documentation clarifying YAML uses snake_case but Elisp uses kebab-case after normalization in jf/gptel-scope--load-config docstring

title: Add documentation explaining base command validation strategy
description:
Both example presets should clearly document that only the BASE COMMAND (first token before pipes/redirects) is categorized and validated.

From scope-shell-tools.org lines 54-64:
'Validation strategy: Only the base command (first command before pipes/redirects) is categorized and validated.'

Current documentation status:

system-explorer.md:
- Lines 210-241: Security model section ✓

- Missing: Explanation that 'brew list' is allowed but 'brew' is the validated command ✗

Issue: Users might not understand that listing 'grep' allows 'grep pattern file | head | tail', or that 'brew' allows 'brew install' (unless paths.read prevents write operations).

Recommendation: Add a 'Command Validation' section to both presets explaining:
1. Only base command is validated
2. Pipes and redirects are allowed if base command is categorized
