title: Verify bash validator error structure matches spec
description:
## Issue

Review the error structure returned by jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool to ensure it matches the spec requirements.

## Spec Requirements (from bash-tools/spec.md)

Error responses should include:
- :error with specific error codes ("command_not_allowed", "command_denied", "dangerous_command", "directory_not_in_scope")
- :tool (tool name)
- :command (the full command)
- :directory (for directory scope errors)
- :required_scope ("read" or "write" for directory errors)
- :allowed_patterns (list of patterns, for directory errors)
- :message (descriptive message suggesting request_scope_expansion)

## Current Implementation

Check lines 596-601, 605-610, 620-627, 651-657, 672-680, 685-694 in scope-core.el for consistency.

## Verification Steps

1. Check error structure has correct keys
2. Verify :command is included (not just base-command)
3. Verify :directory is absolute/real path
4. Verify :required_scope matches category
5. Verify :allowed_patterns are correct for read_only (read+write) vs safe_write (write only)
6. Check that messages suggest request_scope_expansion

## Impact
MEDIUM - Error structure mismatch could confuse LLM or break expansion flow
notes: Reviewed bash validator error structure. Error codes match spec requirements. All required fields present except :resource (tracked in emacs-0kxv). Messages correctly suggest request_scope_expansion.

title: Duplicate pattern matching logic in scope-core and scope-shell-tools
description:
The directory validation logic is duplicated between two files:

1. scope-core.org: jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool (lines 609-735)
   - Contains full validation including path matching
   - Checks deny paths, read paths, write paths
   - Uses jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern

2. scope-shell-tools.org: jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category (lines 202-249)
   - Also validates directory against read/write/deny patterns
   - Uses jf/gptel-scope--matches-any-pattern
   - Essentially duplicates the directory validation logic from validate-bash-tool

This is problematic because:
- Changes to validation logic must be made in two places
- Risk of logic drift between the two implementations
- Line 209 in validate-directory-for-category checks category requirements but this logic is already in validate-bash-tool

Looking at scope-shell-tools.org, these helper functions (jf/gptel-bash--parse-command, jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command, jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category) appear to be UNUSED in the current implementation. The gptel-make-scoped-tool macro in scope-core.org calls jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool directly, which has its own internal logic.

Recommendation: Either remove unused helper functions or refactor validate-bash-tool to use these helpers to avoid duplication.
close_reason: Remove unused duplicate validation helper functions - validation is done in scope-core

title: Implement pattern flow tracking through substitutions
description:
Track how file patterns flow from pattern-producing commands (find, ls, grep -l) through substitutions to outer commands that operate on the matched files.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-recursive.el (add pattern flow tracking)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (extract outer command with substitution context)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-pattern-flow.el (new test file)

Implementation steps:
1. Add pattern flow detection to jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive (parsed-command var-context &optional depth)
     "Recursively extract operations with pattern flow tracking."
     (let ((depth (or depth 0))
           (operations nil)
           (substitution-patterns nil))  ; NEW: Track patterns from substitutions
       
       ;; ... existing depth check ...
       
       ;; Extract operations from current level
       (let ((this-level-ops (jf/bash--extract-from-single-command 
                              parsed-command var-context)))
         (setq operations (append operations this-level-ops)))
       
       ;; Process command substitutions and track pattern output
       (when-let ((substs (plist-get parsed-command :command-substitutions)))
         (dolist (subst substs)
           (when-let ((parsed-subst (plist-get subst :parsed)))
             (when (plist-get parsed-subst :success)
               ;; Recursively analyze substitution
               (let ((subst-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive
                                parsed-subst var-context (1+ depth))))
                 ;; Check if substitution produces a file pattern
                 (let ((pattern-op (seq-find 
                                   (lambda (op) 
                                     (eq (plist-get op :operation) :match-pattern))
                                   subst-ops)))
                   (when pattern-op
                     ;; Track this substitution's pattern output
                     (push (list :substitution-content (plist-get subst :content)
                                :pattern (plist-get pattern-op :file)
                                :search-scope (plist-get pattern-op :search-scope)
                                :command (plist-get pattern-op :command))
                           substitution-patterns)))
                 
                 ;; Mark and append substitution operations
                 (setq subst-ops (mapcar (lambda (op)
                                          (plist-put (copy-sequence op) :from-substitution t)
                                          op)
                                        subst-ops))
                 (setq operations (append operations subst-ops)))))))
       
       ;; Add operations for outer command operating on substitution results
       (when substitution-patterns
         (let ((pattern-flow-ops (jf/bash--extract-pattern-flow-operations
                                 parsed-command substitution-patterns var-context)))
           (setq operations (append operations pattern-flow-ops))))
       
       operations))
   ```

2. Implement jf/bash--extract-pattern-flow-operations in bash-parser-file-ops.el:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash--extract-pattern-flow-operations (parsed-command subst-patterns var-context)
     "Extract operations when outer command receives pattern from substitution.
     
   PARSED-COMMAND is the outer command.
   SUBST-PATTERNS is list of patterns produced by substitutions.
   
   Example: In 'cat \$(find . -name \"*.log\")', this creates a :read operation
   for the *.log pattern since cat receives the find results."
     (let ((operations nil)
           (command-name (plist-get parsed-command :command-name))
           (positional-args (plist-get parsed-command :positional-args)))
       
       ;; Check if outer command has substitutions in positional args
       (dolist (arg positional-args)
         ;; If arg is a substitution (contains $(...))
         (when (string-match-p "\\$(" arg)
           ;; Find matching pattern from substitution
           (dolist (pattern-info subst-patterns)
             (let ((subst-content (plist-get pattern-info :substitution-content)))
               (when (string-match-p (regexp-quote subst-content) arg)
                 ;; Found matching substitution - check outer command semantics
                 (let* ((semantics (jf/bash-lookup-command-semantics command-name))
                        (pattern (plist-get pattern-info :pattern))
                        (search-scope (plist-get pattern-info :search-scope)))
                   
                   ;; If outer command reads from positional args, it reads the pattern
                   (when semantics
                     (let ((ops-spec (plist-get semantics :operations)))
                       (when (and (listp ops-spec)
                                 (seq-find (lambda (spec)
                                            (and (eq (plist-get spec :source) :positional-args)
                                                 (eq (plist-get spec :operation) :read)))
                                          ops-spec))
                         ;; Outer command reads the pattern
                         (push (list :file pattern
                                    :operation :read
                                    :confidence :high
                                    :source :positional-arg
                                    :command command-name
                                    :pattern t
                                    :pattern-source (list :command (plist-get pattern-info :command)
                                                         :search-scope search-scope
                                                         :from-substitution t))
                               operations))))))))))
       
       operations))
   ```

3. Create test-pattern-flow.el with comprehensive pattern flow tests:
   ```elisp
   (ert-deftest test-pattern-flow-cat-find ()
     "Test pattern flows from find to cat."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "cat $(find . -name '*.log')"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; Should have 3 operations:
       ;; 1. find reads directory "."
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) ".")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read-directory)
                               (string= (plist-get op :command) "find")))
                        ops))
       ;; 2. find matches pattern "*.log"
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.log")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :match-pattern)
                               (string= (plist-get op :command) "find")
                               (plist-get op :from-substitution)))
                        ops))
       ;; 3. cat reads pattern "*.log" (pattern flow)
       (let ((cat-op (seq-find (lambda (op)
                                (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.log")
                                     (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read)
                                     (string= (plist-get op :command) "cat")))
                              ops)))
         (should cat-op)
         (should (plist-get cat-op :pattern))
         (should (plist-get cat-op :pattern-source))
         (should (string= (plist-get (plist-get cat-op :pattern-source) :command) "find")))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-pattern-flow-rm-ls ()
     "Test pattern flows from ls glob to rm."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "rm $(ls *.tmp)"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; ls matches pattern "*.tmp"
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.tmp")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :match-pattern)))
                        ops))
       ;; rm deletes pattern "*.tmp"
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.tmp")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :delete)
                               (plist-get op :pattern-source)))
                        ops))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-pattern-flow-nested ()
     "Test pattern flow with nested substitutions."
     (let* ((parsed (jf/bash-parse "cat $(find $(pwd) -name '*.txt')"))
            (ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
       ;; pwd doesn't produce pattern (just outputs directory)
       ;; find produces pattern "*.txt"
       ;; cat reads pattern "*.txt"
       (should (seq-find (lambda (op)
                          (and (string= (plist-get op :file) "*.txt")
                               (eq (plist-get op :operation) :read)
                               (string= (plist-get op :command) "cat")))
                        ops))))
   ```

4. Update test-corpus-file-operations.el with pattern flow expectations:
   ```elisp
   (:id "pattern-flow-001"
    :command "cat $(find . -name '*.log')"
    :note "Cat reads files matched by find"
    :expect-ops ((:file "." :operation :read-directory :command "find")
                 (:file "*.log" :operation :match-pattern :command "find" :pattern t :from-substitution t)
                 (:file "*.log" :operation :read :command "cat" :pattern t :pattern-source (:command "find"))))
   ```

Design rationale: Pattern flow tracking is the key insight that makes nested analysis useful. When find produces `*.log` and cat receives it via substitution, we want the output to show:
1. find searches for *.log (match-pattern)
2. cat reads *.log (read with pattern-source)

This makes it explicit that cat will operate on whatever files find discovers. The :pattern-source field creates a clear link between the pattern producer and the pattern consumer.

Without pattern flow tracking, we'd just see that cat reads a substitution (opaque) and find matches a pattern (disconnected). With it, we see the causal relationship.

Verification:
- Run: make test-bash-parser && make test TEST=test-pattern-flow
- Test: Parse "cat $(find . -name '*.log')" and verify 3 operations with pattern flow
- Test: Parse "rm $(ls *.tmp)" and verify pattern flows from ls to rm
- Test: Parse "cat $(find $(pwd) -name '*.txt')" and verify nested pattern flow
- Verify: cat operation has :pattern-source pointing to find

title: Design Kubernetes operations visitor
description:
## Context

Similar to Docker, kubectl commands perform operations on Kubernetes resources. This visitor will extract pod, deployment, service, and other resource operations.

## Goal

Create a visitor that extracts Kubernetes-specific operations from kubectl commands to demonstrate multi-domain analysis.

## Requirements

1. **Kubectl operations visitor**:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash--kubectl-operations-visitor (node context depth)
     "Extract Kubernetes operations from NODE."
     ;; Returns list of k8s operation plists
     )
   ```

2. **Operation types to extract**:
   - **kubectl apply**: resource file, namespace
   - **kubectl delete**: resource type, resource name, namespace
   - **kubectl create**: resource type, resource name, namespace
   - **kubectl exec**: pod name, namespace, command
   - **kubectl get**: resource type, resource name, namespace
   - **kubectl describe**: resource type, resource name, namespace
   - **kubectl logs**: pod name, namespace, container name

3. **Operation plist format**:
   ```elisp
   (:operation :k8s-apply
    :resource-file "deployment.yaml"
    :namespace "production"
    :command "kubectl"
    :visitor-type 'kubectl-operations
    :confidence :high)
   
   (:operation :k8s-exec
    :pod-name "web-server-abc123"
    :namespace "production"
    :container "app"
    :exec-command ("sh" "-c" "ls /var/log")
    :command "kubectl"
    :visitor-type 'kubectl-operations
    :confidence :high)
   ```

4. **Handle common patterns**:
   - Namespace flags: `-n production`, `--namespace=production`
   - Resource files: `-f deployment.yaml`, `-f dir/`
   - Resource selectors: `pod/web-server`, `deployment/app`
   - Labels: `-l app=web`
   - Container selection: `-c container-name`

## Implementation Plan

1. Create `bash-parser-kubectl.el` module
2. Implement kubectl semantics extractor functions
3. Implement `jf/bash--kubectl-operations-visitor`
4. Add kubectl command patterns to test corpus
5. Write comprehensive tests

## Test Cases

```elisp
;; kubectl apply
"kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml -n production"
=> (:operation :k8s-apply :resource-file "deployment.yaml" 
    :namespace "production" ...)

;; kubectl delete
"kubectl delete pod web-server-abc123 -n production"
=> (:operation :k8s-delete :resource-type "pod" 
    :resource-name "web-server-abc123" :namespace "production" ...)

;; kubectl exec
"kubectl exec -n production web-server-abc123 -- ls /var/log"
=> (:operation :k8s-exec :pod-name "web-server-abc123" 
    :namespace "production" :exec-command ("ls" "/var/log") ...)

;; kubectl logs
"kubectl logs -n production web-server-abc123 -c app"
=> (:operation :k8s-logs :pod-name "web-server-abc123" 
    :namespace "production" :container "app" ...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Extracts apply operations from `kubectl apply`
- [ ] Extracts delete operations from `kubectl delete`
- [ ] Extracts exec operations from `kubectl exec`
- [ ] Parses namespace correctly (`-n`, `--namespace`)
- [ ] Parses resource files (`-f`)
- [ ] Handles resource selectors (pod/name)
- [ ] Works alongside file and docker visitors
- [ ] Comprehensive test coverage

## Files to Create

- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-kubectl.el (new)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-kubectl-visitor.el (new)

## Dependencies

Requires: emacs-six3 (visitor infrastructure)

## Notes

Demonstrates that multiple domain-specific visitors can coexist. A command like:
`kubectl exec pod -- cat /data/config.yaml`

Can be analyzed by:
- kubectl-operations-visitor (pod exec operation)
- file-operations-visitor (cat reading config.yaml inside pod)

Both extracting different facets of the same command.

title: Add bash validation type to scope-core
description:
Add bash validation type to scope-core for scoped bash command execution.

**Context:**
Current scope system validates three tool types:
- path validation (file operations)
- pattern validation (glob-based tools)
- command validation (shell_commands allow list)

Need to add fourth type: bash validation (combines command categorization + directory validation).

**Files to modify:**
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (tangled output)

**Implementation steps:**

1. Add bash validation type to tool categories in scope-core.org:

```elisp
;; In jf/gptel-scope--tool-categories defvar
("run_bash_command" . (:validation bash :operation write))
```

2. Implement jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool validator:

```elisp
(defun jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool (tool-name args config)
  "Validate bash tool: parse command, categorize, validate directory.

ARGS format: (command directory)

Returns (:allowed t) or (:allowed nil :reason ... :allowed-patterns ...)."
  (let* ((command (nth 0 args))
         (directory (nth 1 args))
         (bash-config (plist-get config :bash_tools))
         ;; Parse base command from complex shell string
         (base-cmd (jf/gptel-bash--parse-command command))
         ;; Categorize: deny, read_only, safe_write, dangerous, unknown
         (category (jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command base-cmd bash-config))
         ;; Resolve directory to absolute path
         (abs-dir (file-truename (expand-file-name directory))))
    
    (cond
     ;; Command denied
     ((eq category 'denied)
      (list :allowed nil
            :reason (format "Command '%s' is in deny list" base-cmd)
            :tool tool-name))
     
     ;; Command not in any allow list
     ((eq category 'unknown)
      (list :allowed nil
            :reason (format "Command '%s' not in allowed command lists" base-cmd)
            :tool tool-name
            :message "Use request_scope_expansion to request approval"))
     
     ;; Validate directory for category
     (t
      (jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category abs-dir category config)))))
```

3. Update dispatcher in jf/gptel-scope--validate-tool to handle bash type:

```elisp
(pcase validation-type
  ('path (jf/gptel-scope--validate-path-tool ...))
  ('pattern (jf/gptel-scope--validate-pattern-tool ...))
  ('command (jf/gptel-scope--validate-command-tool ...))
  ('bash (jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool tool-name args config))  ; NEW
  ...)
```

4. Tangle scope-core.org to generate scope-core.el:

```bash
./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/scope/scope-core.org
```

**Design rationale:**
Bash validation combines two concerns: (1) is command allowed? (2) is directory in scope? This differs from existing validators which check only one dimension. Category-based approach makes security model semantic: read_only commands need read paths, safe_write commands need write paths.

**Dependencies:**
This validator calls helper functions implemented in scope-bash-tools.el:
- jf/gptel-bash--parse-command (extracts base command)
- jf/gptel-bash--categorize-command (deny/read_only/safe_write/dangerous/unknown)
- jf/gptel-bash--validate-directory-for-category (checks paths.read/write/deny)

These helpers will be implemented in the next bead.

**Verification:**
- Tangle completes without errors
- check-parens passes
- No missing function references (helpers implemented in next bead)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- scope-core.el contains bash validation type in tool categories
- jf/gptel-scope--validate-bash-tool function defined
- Dispatcher updated to call bash validator
- Code tangles and validates successfully

title: Create scope profile templates and loading functions
description:
Create scope profile templates and loading functions

## Files to create
- `config/gptel/scope-profiles/coding.yml` (NEW)
- `config/gptel/scope-profiles/research.yml` (NEW)
- `config/gptel/scope-profiles/restricted.yml` (NEW)
- `config/gptel/scope-profiles.org` (NEW — tangled to `scope-profiles.el`)

## Implementation steps

1. Create `config/gptel/scope-profiles/` directory
2. Create `coding.yml`:
   ```yaml
   paths:
     read: ["/**"]
     write: ["${project_root}/**"]
     deny: ["**/.git/**", "**/runtime/**", "**/.env", "**/node_modules/**"]
   org_roam_patterns:
     subdirectory: ["gptel/**"]
   shell_commands:
     allow: ["ls", "find", "grep", "git", "rg"]
     deny: ["rm -rf", "sudo"]
   ```
3. Create `research.yml` — broad read (`/**`), no write section, standard deny, org-roam read patterns
4. Create `restricted.yml` — minimal read permissions, no write, standard deny
5. Create `config/gptel/scope-profiles.org` as a standalone module (NOT in preset-registration.org):
   - Provides `gptel-scope-profiles` feature
   - Loaded by `gptel.org` after preset-registration (needs `jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults` from preset-registration)
6. Add `jf/gptel-scope-profile--load`:
   - Read `.yml` file from scope-profiles directory
   - Parse entire content with `yaml-parse-string` (no frontmatter — plain YAML)
   - Apply snake_case → kebab-case key normalization
   - Return plist with `:paths`, `:org-roam-patterns`, `:shell-commands`
7. Add `jf/gptel-scope-profile--resolve`:
   - Given a preset name, look up `jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults`
   - Priority: named profile (`:scope-profile` key) → inline scope defaults → empty/deny-by-default
   - If named profile specified, call `--load` to read the profile template
   - If profile file missing, log warning, return empty scope
8. Add `jf/gptel-scope-profile--expand-variables`:
   - Replace `${project_root}` in all string values with actual path
   - Resolve from `projectile-project-root` or fallback
   - If no project detected, remove patterns containing `${project_root}` and log warning
9. Add `jf/gptel-scope-profile--write-scope-yml`:
   - Write resolved scope plist as plain YAML to a given directory path
   - File is `scope.yml` in the target directory
   - Support explicit paths (from activities worktree-paths) that bypass template expansion
10. Tangle and validate

## Design rationale

Scope profiles are a standalone capability separate from preset registration. While scope profiles read from `jf/gptel-preset--scope-defaults` (populated by preset-registration), the loading/resolution/expansion logic is independent and warrants its own module for clean separation. This matches the OpenSpec delta spec which designates scope-profiles as a NEW capability.

Separates mutable per-session scope from immutable preset definitions. Profile templates use `${project_root}` resolved at session creation time from `projectile-project-root`. Plain YAML (no frontmatter) is simpler to parse and serialize. Three default profiles cover common use cases: coding (full access), research (read-only), restricted (minimal).

## Verification

- Profile files parse without error via `yaml-parse-string`
- `(jf/gptel-scope-profile--resolve 'executor)` returns expected scope plist
- `(jf/gptel-scope-profile--expand-variables plist "/Users/jeff/project")` replaces `${project_root}` correctly
- `jf/gptel-scope-profile--write-scope-yml` writes valid YAML readable by `yaml-parse-string`
- Missing profile logs warning and returns empty scope

Context: design.md § Decision 4, scope-profiles/spec.md

title: scope-expansion parses YAML without normalization but expects hyphenated keys
description:
**Issue:** jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope parses YAML without normalization (correctly), but line 371 uses hyphenated category names (:read-only, :safe-write) when the parsed YAML has underscored names (:read_only, :safe_write).

**Root cause:** Lines 354-360 parse YAML without normalization (CORRECT):
```elisp
(parsed (condition-case err
           (yaml-parse-string content
                              :object-type 'plist
                              :sequence-type 'list)
         ...))
```

This returns underscored keys like :bash_tools, :read_only, :safe_write (matching YAML format).

Lines 362-365 correctly use underscored keys:
```elisp
(bash-tools (or (plist-get parsed :bash-tools)      ;; Correct
                (plist-get parsed :bash_tools)))     ;; Defensive fallback
(categories (or (plist-get bash-tools :categories))) ;; Correct
```

But line 371 INCORRECTLY uses hyphenated category names:
```elisp
(target-category (if (eq operation 'read) :read-only :safe-write)) ;; WRONG
```

Should be:
```elisp
(target-category (if (eq operation 'read) :read_only :safe_write)) ;; Correct
```

**Impact:** The code will fail to find existing categories in parsed YAML because it's looking for :read-only when the key is :read_only. Any bash command expansion will create NEW category entries with hyphenated names instead of updating existing underscored entries, leading to duplicate/malformed YAML structure.

**Expected behavior:** Use underscored category names throughout jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope since YAML is not normalized:
- Line 371: Use :read_only instead of :read-only
- Line 371: Use :safe_write instead of :safe-write
- Update comment on line 370 to reflect this

**Files affected:**
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.org (line 371: target-category assignment)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-expansion.el (generated)

**Note:** This is a CRITICAL bug that breaks bash command expansion. The code will create malformed YAML with both underscored and hyphenated category keys.

title: Update activities extension to use gptel--known-presets
description:
Update activities extension to use gptel--known-presets

## Files to modify
- `config/gptel/sessions/activities-integration.org`

## Implementation steps

1. Replace preset listing source:
   - Change `(fboundp 'jf/gptel--list-preset-templates)` guard to `(bound-and-true-p gptel--known-presets)`
   - Replace filesystem scan with `(mapcar #'car gptel--known-presets)` for preset name listing
   - Show `:description` from each preset as completion annotation
2. Update preset parameter passing:
   - Pass preset name symbol (e.g., `'executor`) to session creation — NOT a template filename
   - Session creation function receives symbol and writes it to metadata.yml
3. Update session creation call:
   - Ensure the activities integration calls `jf/gptel--create-session-core` with the preset name symbol
   - The creation function handles scope.yml writing (see Bead 6)
4. Multi-project scope from worktree paths:
   - When `worktree-paths` are provided, pass them to session creation
   - Session creation writes explicit paths to scope.yml (bypassing profile template expansion)
   - Each worktree path gets `/**` glob suffix for read entries and write entries
   - Standard deny entries always added (`.git`, `runtime`, `.env`, `node_modules`)
   - Does NOT use `${project_root}` expansion (paths are already resolved)
5. Tangle and validate with `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/sessions/activities-integration.org`

## Design rationale

Activities integration now uses registered presets as the authoritative source instead of filesystem scanning. This is faster and guaranteed consistent with what the session system uses. Multi-project scope from worktree paths is written directly to scope.yml — these are already-resolved absolute paths from the activities system that shouldn't go through `${project_root}` template expansion.

The data contract between activities and gptel sessions is unchanged: the plist stored in activity metadata still has `:session-id`, `:session-file`, `:session-dir`. Session resume via `jf/gptel-session--open-existing` still opens the session.md file, which triggers the find-file-hook.

## Verification

- `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/sessions/activities-integration.org` passes
- Activity creation shows registered presets with descriptions in completion
- Activity with gptel session creates session with correct `scope.yml` and `metadata.yml`
- Multi-project session with worktree paths: `scope.yml` contains explicit paths (no `${project_root}`)
- Activity resume works: opens session.md, find-file-hook handles initialization
- No references to `jf/gptel--list-preset-templates` remain

Presets reference scope profiles (not inline bash_tools) to demonstrate the recommended architecture pattern. System messages educate LLM about category-based validation and best practices (explicit directory, use filters for large output).

title: [HIGH] PersistentAgent: Empty session.md not created upfront - only after first auto-save
description:
## Problem

Spec requires creating empty session.md file during agent creation, but implementation only associates buffer with file path without creating the file.

## Spec Requirement

**openspec/specs/gptel/persistent-agent.md lines 110-113:**
> Scenario: Empty session.md created
> - WHEN creating an agent session
> - THEN the system creates an empty `session.md` file
> - AND associates the file with the agent buffer via set-visited-file-name

## Current Implementation

**config/gptel/tools/persistent-agent.org lines 681-683:**
```elisp
;; Associate buffer with file
(set-visited-file-name (jf/gptel--context-file-path session-dir))
(set-buffer-modified-p t)
```

Only calls set-visited-file-name, which does NOT create the file on disk.

## Impact

- Agent directory initially contains only: preset.md, scope-plan.yml
- session.md doesn't exist until first auto-save after first API response
- Directory structure incomplete until first auto-save
- Could break tools expecting session.md to exist immediately after creation
- Timing differs from spec

## Fix

Create empty file before associating:
```elisp
;; Create empty session.md and associate buffer with file
(let ((session-file (jf/gptel--context-file-path session-dir)))
  (with-temp-file session-file
    (insert ""))  ; Create empty file
  (set-visited-file-name session-file)
  (set-buffer-modified-p nil))  ; File exists and is empty, not modified
```

## Verification

Agent ID: ab7db55

title: [MINOR] PersistentAgent: Documentation describes spec intent not implementation for empty array behavior
description:

Documentation claims empty array allows unrestricted access, but implementation inherits from parent.

## Documentation

**config/gptel/tools/persistent-agent.org lines 566-567:**
> If specified as empty array [], agent has no path restrictions (reads from anywhere).

## Actual Implementation Behavior

**Lines 598-616:**
Empty array converts to nil and falls through to parent inheritance logic.

## Impact

- Documentation misleads users about empty array behavior
- Users following documentation will have incorrect expectations
- Documentation describes spec intention, not actual behavior

## Fix Options

**Option 1:** Update documentation to match implementation:
> If omitted or specified as empty array [], agent inherits allowed paths from parent.

**Option 2:** Fix implementation to match documentation (see bead for empty array path behavior).

## Related

This is a documentation consequence of the empty array path behavior delta. Consider resolving both together.

title: Glob Pattern Matcher - Core Algorithm
description:
Implement segment-based glob pattern matching without filesystem access.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "Glob Pattern Matching"
2. Implement path/pattern segmentation (split by "/" separator)
3. Implement glob-to-regex conversion:
   - "*" → ".*" (match any chars within segment, not crossing /)
   - "**" → special handling (matches 0-N complete segments)
   - "?" → "." (single character)
   - "[abc]" → "[abc]" (character class preserved)
   - Escape regex special chars in literals
4. Implement segment matching algorithm:
   - Match segments one-by-one
   - "**" can consume zero or more segments
   - Single "*" does not cross directory boundaries
5. Implement `jf/bash-glob-match-p` main function

Design rationale:
Port Python's glob.translate() algorithm because it's proven (production use worldwide) and handles ** correctly. Segment-based matching is critical - single * matches within segment only, ** matches across segments. No filesystem access needed - works on paths that don't exist yet (pre-validation). Pure string operations and regex for performance.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-glob-match-p (path pattern)
  "Match PATH against PATTERN using glob semantics. No filesystem access."
  (let ((path-segments (split-string path "/" t))
        (pattern-segments (split-string pattern "/" t)))
    (jf/bash--match-segments path-segments pattern-segments)))

(defun jf/bash--match-segments (path-segs pattern-segs)
  "Recursively match segments. Handle ** consuming 0-N segments."
  (cond
   ((and (null path-segs) (null pattern-segs)) t)
   ((string= (car pattern-segs) "**")
    ;; Try matching 0, 1, 2, ... segments
    (or (jf/bash--match-segments path-segs (cdr pattern-segs))
        (and path-segs (jf/bash--match-segments (cdr path-segs) pattern-segs))))
   ((and path-segs pattern-segs)
    (and (string-match-p (jf/bash--glob-to-regex (car pattern-segs)) (car path-segs))
         (jf/bash--match-segments (cdr path-segs) (cdr pattern-segs))))
   (t nil)))
```

Verification:
- "/workspace/file.txt" matches "/workspace/*.txt" ✓
- "/workspace/src/foo.el" matches "/workspace/**/*.el" ✓
- "/workspace/file.txt" matches "/workspace/**/file.txt" (** = 0 segments) ✓
- "/tmp/foo.el" does NOT match "/workspace/**/*.el" ✓
- Test all scenarios from bash-sandbox-security/spec.md glob sections

Context: design.md § "Decision 3: Glob Pattern Matching Algorithm"
close_reason: Implemented glob pattern matcher with segment-based algorithm. All test cases passing.

title: Command substitution creates duplicate operation
description:
Test: test-corpus-integration-002
Command: cat $(find . -name '*.txt') 2> errors.log

Expected 4 operations, got 5. The issue is an extra operation for the command substitution itself:

Extra operation: (:file "$(find . -name '*.txt')" :operation :read :confidence :high :source :positional-arg :command "cat" :pattern t)

This is in addition to the 4 correct operations:
1. (:file "." :operation :read-directory :command "find" :from-substitution t)
2. (:file "*.txt" :operation :match-pattern :command "find" :pattern t :from-substitution t)
3. (:file "*.txt" :operation :read :command "cat" :pattern t :pattern-source ...)
4. (:file "errors.log" :operation :write :source :redirection)

The substitution text should not itself be treated as a file operation.

Files to investigate:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org (positional arg processing)
- Look for how arguments containing $(...) are handled
- Check if substitutions are being skipped when extracting from positional args

Implementation:
1. Find where positional args are processed for file operations
2. Add check: if arg contains command/process substitution, skip it as direct file op
3. The substitution contents should already be processed recursively
4. Ensure only the recursive operations are kept, not the substitution text itself

Design rationale: Command substitutions are not file paths themselves - they're code that produces values. The file operations come from executing the substituted command, not from treating the substitution text as a path.

Verification:
- Run make test-bash-parser
- Confirm test-corpus-integration-002 passes
- Verify 4 operations, not 5
- Test nested substitutions don't create duplicate operations

title: Update session branching to copy scope.yml/metadata.yml
description:
Update session branching to copy scope.yml/metadata.yml

## Files to modify
- `config/gptel/sessions/branching.org`

## Implementation steps

1. Update `jf/gptel--create-branch-session` config inheritance:
   - Copy `scope.yml` from parent branch directory (via `jf/gptel--scope-file-path`)
   - Copy `metadata.yml` from parent branch directory (via `jf/gptel--metadata-file-path`)

2. Create `branch-metadata.yml` in new branch directory with:
   - `parent_branch`: parent branch name
   - `created`: ISO8601 timestamp
   - `branch_point_position`: position in conversation (optional, if branch created from specific point)
3. Add backward compatibility for legacy parent branches:
   - If parent has `scope-plan.yml` and `preset.md` but no `scope.yml`/`metadata.yml`, copy legacy files as-is
   - Do NOT attempt to migrate during branch creation
4. Verify auto-initialization works: opening new branch's `session.md` triggers the find-file-hook (no separate initialization path needed for branches)
5. Tangle and validate with `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/sessions/branching.org`

## Design rationale

Branches copy mutable scope config (scope.yml) and session metadata (metadata.yml). The preset definition lives in `gptel--known-presets` and is referenced by name in the Local Variables — no need to copy it. Backward compat copies legacy files as-is rather than migrating during branch creation — migration happens at session open time (see Bead 5).

## Verification

- `./bin/tangle-org.sh config/gptel/sessions/branching.org` passes
- Branch from new-format session: copies `scope.yml` and `metadata.yml`, creates `branch-metadata.yml`
- Branch from legacy session: copies `scope-plan.yml` and `preset.md` as-is
- Opening branch session.md triggers find-file-hook correctly
- Scope modifications in branch do NOT affect parent's `scope.yml`

Context: design.md § Migration Phase 2, sessions-branching.md

title: Review package manager commands for write operations
description:
system-explorer.md includes package manager commands in read_only category, but package managers support both read and write operations:

Read-only operations (safe):
- brew list, brew info, brew search
- apt list, apt show, apt search
- pip list, pip show, pip search
- npm list, npm view, npm search

Write operations (require scope expansion or safe_write):
- brew install, brew uninstall, brew upgrade
- apt install, apt remove, apt update
- pip install, pip uninstall
- npm install, npm uninstall

Current listing (system-explorer.md lines 56-65):
Lists bare command names: 'brew', 'apt', 'yum', 'dnf', 'pip', 'pip3', 'npm', 'gem', 'cargo'

The documentation correctly states these are read-only queries (line 231-234), but the command parsing only looks at the first token.

Example concern:
- Command: 'brew install tree'
- Parser extracts: 'brew'
- Category: read_only ✓
- Scope requirement: paths.read
- But this MODIFIES system state!

However, reading scope-shell-tools.org lines 61-64, only the BASE COMMAND is validated. So 'brew' in read_only list means 'brew <anything>' is allowed.

This is a potential security issue. Either:
1. Package managers should NOT be in read_only (too permissive)
2. Documentation should warn about subcommand risks
3. Parser should validate subcommands for known dangerous commands

Recommendation: Move package managers out of read_only category into a commented section with warning, or add explicit subcommand parsing for package managers.

File: /Users/jefffarr/emacs/config/gptel/presets/system-explorer.md
Lines: 56-65
notes: Reviewed package manager command handling. Found security issue - created bead emacs-3bwx to track the fix.

title: Profile templates lack explanatory comments about categories and deny lists
description: The scope profile templates (research.yml, bash-enabled.yml, coding.yml) have bash_tools sections with categories and deny lists, but no YAML comments explaining: 1) Why commands are categorized as read_only vs safe_write, 2) Why specific commands are in deny lists, 3) Security rationale for empty dangerous category. Templates should have inline comments similar to bash-tools-example.md which has excellent explanatory comments (lines 28-74). This is especially important for templates since they serve as examples.

title: Fix bash-tools spec: scope.yml not preset.md
description:
## Problem

The bash-tools/spec.md delta spec incorrectly states that scope expansion adds commands to preset.md, but the actual implementation updates scope.yml.

Line: 206

```

## Reality

Implementation in scope-expansion.org (line 127) and scope-expansion.el (line 84) routes to jf/gptel-scope--add-bash-to-scope, which updates scope.yml in the session's branch directory, NOT preset.md.

Design.md explicitly states (line 57): "The integration layer changed from preset.md to scope.yml"

- **THEN** the system adds command to appropriate category in scope.yml bash_tools section

2. Cross-check with actual implementation in scope-expansion.org

title: Fix bash-parser-variables void-variable error in chain extraction
description:
**Problem:**

When running tests after implementing recursive semantic analyzer (emacs-r8ge), tests that use variable assignment in chains (e.g., `DIR=/tmp && cat $DIR/file.txt`) fail with:

(void-variable DIR=/tmp)
```

**Investigation:**

- File is NOT byte-compiled (checked thoroughly)

**Impact:**

- 44+ existing tests also fail

**Next steps:**

2. Check for symbol table pollution

**Note:** Discovered during emacs-r8ge. Core bead functionality is complete and working.

title: Document visitor pattern architecture
description:
## Context

With the visitor pattern implemented, we need comprehensive documentation explaining the architecture, how to create new visitors, and how to use the approval API.

## Goal

Create documentation that explains the visitor pattern refactoring, guides developers in creating new visitors, and documents the approval API for LLM integration.

## Requirements

1. **Architecture overview document** (README-visitor-pattern.md):
   - Explain the visitor pattern and why we use it
   - Show the separation between traversal and analysis
   - Diagram the visitor flow
   - Contrast with previous recursive analyzer approach
   - Explain extensibility benefits

2. **Visitor development guide** (VISITOR-GUIDE.md):
   - Visitor function contract
   - Step-by-step guide to creating a new visitor
   - Example: simple network operations visitor
   - Best practices for visitor implementation
   - Testing strategies for visitors

3. **Approval API documentation** (README-approval.md):
   - How to integrate with LLM tools
   - Risk scoring rules and rationale
   - Examples of approval decisions
   - How to customize risk rules
   - Integration examples

4. **Update main README**:
   - Add visitor pattern section
   - Link to new documentation
   - Update architecture diagram
   - Add approval API examples

## Implementation Plan

1. Create README-visitor-pattern.md with architecture overview
2. Create VISITOR-GUIDE.md with development guide
3. Create README-approval.md with LLM integration docs
4. Update main README.md with links and examples
5. Add code examples and diagrams
6. Review with fresh eyes for clarity

## Content Outline

### README-visitor-pattern.md
```markdown
# Visitor Pattern Architecture

## Overview
Why visitor pattern, what problem it solves

## Architecture
- Parser → AST
- Visitor infrastructure → traversal
- Domain visitors → extraction
- Approval API → decision

## Benefits
- Extensibility
- Separation of concerns
- Composability
- Testability

## Visitor Types
- file-operations-visitor
- docker-operations-visitor
- kubectl-operations-visitor
- (custom visitors)

## Flow Diagram
[ASCII or mermaid diagram showing flow]
```

### VISITOR-GUIDE.md
```markdown
# Creating Custom Visitors

## Visitor Contract
Function signature, return format

## Step-by-Step Guide
1. Create module file
2. Define visitor function
3. Implement extraction logic
4. Register with visitor system
5. Write tests

## Example: Network Operations Visitor
Complete working example

## Best Practices
- Keep visitors focused
- Use semantics database when possible
- Test in isolation
- Document operation formats
```

### README-approval.md
```markdown
# LLM Command Approval API

## Overview
How the approval system works

## Risk Levels
:safe, :low, :medium, :high, :critical

## Integration Example
Code showing how to use jf/bash-analyze-for-approval

## Risk Scoring Rules
Detailed explanation of each level

## Customization
How to adjust risk rules

## Multi-domain Analysis
How visitors compose
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] README-visitor-pattern.md explains architecture clearly
- [ ] VISITOR-GUIDE.md provides step-by-step guide
- [ ] README-approval.md documents LLM integration
- [ ] Main README.md updated with links
- [ ] Code examples are tested and working
- [ ] Diagrams clarify architecture
- [ ] Fresh reader can understand and create visitors
- [ ] Documentation is discoverable

## Files to Create

- config/experiments/bash-parser/README-visitor-pattern.md (new)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/VISITOR-GUIDE.md (new)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/README-approval.md (new)

## Files to Modify

- config/experiments/bash-parser/README.md (update)

## Dependencies

Requires:
- emacs-six3 (infrastructure to document)
- emacs-c4ho (file-ops visitor example)
- emacs-qa6o (approval API to document)

Nice to have (better examples):
- emacs-5gur (docker visitor example)
- emacs-t2h8 (kubectl visitor example)

## Notes

Good documentation is critical for maintainability and extensibility. Focus on:
- **Why** we use visitor pattern (not just what)
- **How** to extend with new visitors
- **Examples** showing real usage

Documentation should enable someone unfamiliar with the codebase to create a new visitor (e.g., terraform-operations-visitor) without needing to ask questions.

title: Inconsistent command parsing between scope-core and scope-shell-tools
description:
## Issue

Two different command parsers exist with inconsistent behavior:

### scope-shell-tools.el (lines 31-42)
```elisp
(defun jf/gptel-bash--parse-command (cmd-string)
  (let* ((trimmed (string-trim cmd-string))
         (parts (split-string trimmed "[ |><;&]+" t))  ; ← + quantifier
         (base (car parts)))
    base))
```

### scope-core.el (lines 568-570)
```elisp
(let* ((command-parts (split-string command-full "[ |><;&]" t))  ; ← No + quantifier
       (base-command (car command-parts)))
```

## Differences

1. **Trimming**: scope-shell-tools trims whitespace, scope-core doesn't
2. **Quantifier**: scope-shell-tools uses + (one or more), scope-core doesn't
3. **Duplication**: Logic duplicated instead of reusing helper

## Edge Cases Affected

Without + quantifier:
- "ls  -la" (double space) might behave differently
- "ls && cd" vs "ls&&cd" treated differently

Without trimming:
- "  ls -la" (leading space) might fail to extract command correctly

## Impact

MEDIUM - Inconsistent behavior could cause commands to be incorrectly categorized or denied.

## Solution

Refactor scope-core.el line 569-570 to use jf/gptel-bash--parse-command helper:
```elisp
(let* ((base-command (jf/gptel-bash--parse-command command-full)))
```

This ensures consistent parsing and reduces code duplication.

## Files
- config/gptel/tools/scope-shell-tools.el (lines 31-42)
- config/gptel/scope/scope-core.el (lines 568-570)
close_reason: Fixed inconsistent command parsing: aligned split-string regex in scope-core.org bash validator to use '[ |><;&]+' (with +) to match scope-shell-tools.org parsing logic. Both now consistently split on one or more shell metacharacters.

title: Verify command substitution skip logic doesn't break other recursive tests
description:
Comprehensive verification that Agent 2's command substitution skip logic is correct and doesn't cause other failures.

**CONTEXT:**

Agent 2 (emacs-vcrm) added skip logic in bash-parser-file-ops.org to prevent command substitutions from being treated as literal file paths:

```elisp
;; Skip command substitutions - they are processed recursively
;; and handled by pattern flow operations
(unless (and file-path (string-prefix-p "$(" file-path))
  ...)
```

Location: config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.org, function jf/bash--extract-ops-from-positional-specs

This fixed emacs-vcrm (duplicate operation bug) but broke test-recursive-single-substitution due to test expectation mismatch.

**VERIFICATION TASKS:**

1. **Run Full Test Suite:**
   - Execute: make test-directory DIR=config/experiments/bash-parser
   - Verify test-recursive-single-substitution is the ONLY failure
   - Document any other failures and investigate if related

2. **Review Other Recursive Tests:**
   Files to check:
   - config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-bash-parser-recursive.el (all 16 tests)
   - config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-command-substitution.el
   - config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-pattern-flow.el
   
   Verify none have similar assertions expecting :file to contain $(...)

3. **Test Pattern Flow Scenarios:**
   Verify pattern flow operations work correctly for:
   - cat $(find . -name '*.log')
   - rm $(grep -l pattern *.txt)
   - cp $(ls *.bak) /backup/
   
   Each should create pattern flow operation with correct :pattern-source

4. **Test Edge Cases:**
   - Multiple substitutions: cat $(find ...) $(grep ...)
   - Nested substitutions: cat $(find $(pwd) ...)
   - Substitution with flags: cat -n $(find ...)
   - Substitution in middle: tar -czf backup.tar.gz $(find . -name '*.txt')

5. **Verify Original Bug is Fixed:**
   Check that the duplicate operation issue (emacs-vcrm) stays fixed:
   - Command should NOT create operation for literal "$(find ...)" string
   - Should NOT appear in security validation as a file path
   - Pattern flow should handle the semantic relationship

Files to verify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-bash-parser-recursive.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-command-substitution.el  
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-pattern-flow.el
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-corpus-file-operations.el

Implementation steps:
1. Run make test-directory DIR=config/experiments/bash-parser and capture output
2. Count failures and identify which tests fail
3. For each failure, determine if caused by skip logic or test expectation issue
4. Review test assertions in recursive tests for similar patterns to the failing test
5. Test edge cases manually with jf/bash-parse and jf/bash-extract-file-operations
6. Document findings in bead comments

Verification:
- Test suite should have 463/467 passing after emacs-6dpn fix (fixing test-recursive-single-substitution)
- No other tests should fail due to skip logic
- Edge cases should work correctly
- Original bug (duplicate operations) should stay fixed

Design rationale:
Before merging the fix for emacs-6dpn, we need confidence that Agent 2's change is architecturally sound. This comprehensive verification ensures the skip logic is correct and the regression was purely a test expectation issue, not a symptom of deeper problems.

Dependencies:
- Should be done AFTER emacs-6dpn fix to verify the fix is sufficient
- Blocks final validation that test suite is healthy

Related beads:
- emacs-6dpn (the fix this verifies)
- emacs-vcrm (the original bug Agent 2 was fixing)
close_reason: Verification complete - all 467 tests passed after emacs-6dpn fix (commit 6ecbfac). Command substitution skip logic confirmed working correctly with no regressions in recursive tests. Test suite has since expanded to 496 tests with one unrelated failure in variable resolution.

title: Update sessions persistence to use three-phase pipeline
description:
Refactor session initialization in commands.org to use parse → resolve → apply pipeline instead of monolithic preset loading.

Files to modify:
- config/gptel/sessions/commands.org

Implementation steps:
1. Locate jf/gptel--session--open-hook function
2. Replace preset loading logic with three-phase pipeline:
   ```elisp
   (when-let* ((preset-path (expand-file-name "preset.md" branch-dir))
               (parsed (jf/gptel-preset-parse preset-path))
               (resolved (jf/gptel-preset-resolve parsed)))
     (jf/gptel-preset-apply resolved))
   ```
3. Update error handling to check for nil after parsing (graceful fallback)
4. Locate preset serialization during session creation
5. Replace jf/gptel--normalize-preset-for-serialization with jf/gptel-preset-serialize
6. Remove deprecated functions:
   - Delete jf/gptel--load-preset-from-file
   - Delete jf/gptel--apply-session-preset
   - Delete jf/gptel--normalize-preset-for-serialization
7. Grep codebase to ensure no remaining references to deleted functions

Design rationale:
Three-phase pipeline makes data flow visible at call sites. Sessions use graceful error handling (nil checks) because preset loading failure should not crash session opening - user can still interact with session using defaults. Explicit phases make debugging easier (can inspect parsed or resolved data).

Design pattern:
```elisp
;; In session open hook
(when-let* ((preset-path (expand-file-name "preset.md" branch-dir))
            (parsed (jf/gptel-preset-parse preset-path)))
  (condition-case err
      (jf/gptel-preset-apply (jf/gptel-preset-resolve parsed))
    (error
     (message "Preset resolution failed: %s. Using defaults." (error-message-string err)))))
```

Verification:
- Tangle both files and validate
- Open existing session → verify backend/model/tools/system loaded correctly
- Create new session → verify preset.md written in canonical format
- Parse preset with missing tools → verify warnings logged, partial list used
- Parse preset with invalid YAML → verify graceful fallback to defaults
- Grep for deprecated function names → should find zero references

Context: design.md § Decision 5: Update call sites to use new architecture, § Migration Plan step 2

title: Update main entry point and ensure backward compatibility
description:
Update the main jf/bash-extract-file-operations entry point to use recursive analysis, ensure backward compatibility with existing tests, and update documentation.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (update main entry point)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.el (update requires and docs)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/test/test-bash-parser.el (verify backward compatibility)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/README.md (update documentation if exists)

Implementation steps:
1. Update jf/bash-extract-file-operations to use recursive analyzer:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash-extract-file-operations (parsed-command &optional var-context)
     "Extract all file operations from PARSED-COMMAND.
     
   This is the main entry point for file operation extraction. Now uses
   recursive semantic analysis to extract operations from all nesting levels.
   
   PARSED-COMMAND is the output from `jf/bash-parse'.
   VAR-CONTEXT is an optional alist mapping variable names (symbols or strings) to values.
   
   Returns a flat list of operation plists, each containing:
     :file - File path (may contain unresolved variables or patterns)
     :operation - Operation type (:read, :write, :delete, :modify, :create, :append,
                                  :match-pattern, :read-directory, :read-metadata)
     :confidence - Confidence level (:high, :medium, :low)
     :source - Source of operation (:redirection, :positional-arg, :exec-block, etc.)
     :command - Command name that performs the operation
     
     Context flags (optional):
     :from-substitution - t if from command substitution
     :loop-context - t if in loop body
     :loop-variable - Loop variable name if in loop
     :conditional - t if in conditional branch
     :branch - :then or :else if in conditional
     :test-condition - t if in conditional test
     :pattern - t if file path is a glob pattern
     :pattern-source - Plist identifying pattern producer if applicable
     :heredoc-content - t if from heredoc with redirect
     :indirect - t if from nested/indirect execution
     
   Handles arbitrary nesting depth up to 10 levels (configurable).
   Operations are deduplicated by file+operation pair.
   
   Examples:
     Simple: (jf/bash-extract-file-operations (jf/bash-parse \"cat file.txt\"))
     => ((:file \"file.txt\" :operation :read :confidence :high :command \"cat\"))
     
     Nested: (jf/bash-extract-file-operations (jf/bash-parse \"cat \$(find . -name '*.log')\"))
     => ((:file \".\" :operation :read-directory :command \"find\" :from-substitution t)
         (:file \"*.log\" :operation :match-pattern :command \"find\" :pattern t :from-substitution t)
         (:file \"*.log\" :operation :read :command \"cat\" :pattern t :pattern-source (...)))
     
     Loop: (jf/bash-extract-file-operations (jf/bash-parse \"for f in *.txt; do rm \$f; done\"))
     => ((:file \"*.txt\" :operation :delete :command \"rm\" :pattern t :loop-context t))"
     ;; Normalize var-context
     (let ((context (jf/bash--normalize-var-context var-context)))
       ;; Use recursive analyzer (requires bash-parser-recursive)
       (require 'bash-parser-recursive)
       (let ((all-ops (jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive 
                      parsed-command context 0)))
         ;; Deduplicate and return
         (jf/bash--deduplicate-operations all-ops))))
   ```

2. Verify backward compatibility with existing tests:
   ```elisp
   ;; In test-bash-parser.el, add backward compatibility verification
   (ert-deftest test-backward-compat-simple-commands ()
     "Verify recursive analyzer maintains backward compatibility for simple commands."
     (let ((test-cases '(("cat file.txt" 
                         ((:file "file.txt" :operation :read)))
                        ("rm temp.txt"
                         ((:file "temp.txt" :operation :delete)))
                        ("cp src.txt dst.txt"
                         ((:file "src.txt" :operation :read)
                          (:file "dst.txt" :operation :write))))))
       (dolist (case test-cases)
         (let* ((command (car case))
                (expected-ops (cadr case))
                (parsed (jf/bash-parse command))
                (actual-ops (jf/bash-extract-file-operations parsed)))
           ;; Verify expected operations are present (may have additional context flags)
           (dolist (expected-op expected-ops)
             (should (seq-find (lambda (actual-op)
                                (and (string= (plist-get actual-op :file)
                                             (plist-get expected-op :file))
                                     (eq (plist-get actual-op :operation)
                                        (plist-get expected-op :operation))))
                              actual-ops)))))))
   
   (ert-deftest test-backward-compat-existing-corpus ()
     "Verify all existing test-corpus-file-operations tests still pass."
     ;; Load and run existing corpus tests
     (require 'test-corpus-file-operations)
     ;; All existing tests should pass without modification
     (ert-run-tests-batch "test-corpus-"))
   ```

3. Update module loading in bash-parser.el:
   ```elisp
   ;;; bash-parser.el --- Main entry point for bash parser
   
   (require 'bash-parser-core)         ; Core tree-sitter parsing
   (require 'bash-parser-semantics)    ; Command semantics database
   (require 'bash-parser-file-ops)     ; File operations extraction
   (require 'bash-parser-variables)    ; Variable resolution
   (require 'bash-parser-recursive)    ; Recursive semantic analysis (NEW)
   (require 'bash-parser-extensions)   ; Optional extensions
   
   (provide 'bash-parser)
   ```

4. Add feature detection for new capabilities:
   ```elisp
   (defun jf/bash-parser-has-feature-p (feature)
     "Check if bash-parser has FEATURE enabled.
     
   Features:
     :recursive-analysis - Recursive semantic analysis
     :pattern-flow - Pattern flow tracking through substitutions
     :loop-context - Loop variable binding and context tracking
     :conditional-context - Conditional branch context tracking
     :heredoc-context - Heredoc context disambiguation
     
   Returns t if feature is available, nil otherwise."
     (pcase feature
       (:recursive-analysis (fboundp 'jf/bash-analyze-file-operations-recursive))
       (:pattern-flow (fboundp 'jf/bash--extract-pattern-flow-operations))
       (:loop-context (fboundp 'jf/bash--resolve-loop-variable))
       (:conditional-context (fboundp 'jf/bash--extract-file-test-operations))
       (:heredoc-context (fboundp 'jf/bash--determine-heredoc-context))
       (_ nil)))
   ```

5. Update documentation with new capabilities:
   ```markdown
   # Bash Parser - File Operations Extraction
   
   ## Overview
   
   Extracts file operations from bash commands using tree-sitter parsing
   and recursive semantic analysis.
   
   ## Features
   
   - **Recursive Analysis**: Extracts operations from nested command substitutions
   - **Pattern Flow Tracking**: Tracks how file patterns flow from producers (find, ls)
     to consumers (cat, rm)
   - **Loop Context**: Resolves loop variables to understand batch operations
   - **Conditional Context**: Marks conditional operations and test conditions
   - **Heredoc Context**: Distinguishes file creation from content provision
   
   ## Usage
   
   ```elisp
   ;; Simple command
   (jf/bash-extract-file-operations (jf/bash-parse "cat file.txt"))
   => ((:file "file.txt" :operation :read :confidence :high :command "cat"))
   
   ;; Nested substitution with pattern flow
   (jf/bash-extract-file-operations (jf/bash-parse "cat $(find . -name '*.log')"))
   => ((:file "." :operation :read-directory :from-substitution t)
       (:file "*.log" :operation :match-pattern :pattern t :from-substitution t)
       (:file "*.log" :operation :read :pattern t :pattern-source (:command "find")))
   
   ;; Loop with variable resolution
   (jf/bash-extract-file-operations (jf/bash-parse "for f in *.txt; do rm $f; done"))
   => ((:file "*.txt" :operation :delete :loop-context t :pattern t))
   ```
   
   ## Operation Types
   
   - `:read` - Read file contents
   - `:write` - Create or overwrite file
   - `:delete` - Remove file
   - `:modify` - Modify file in-place
   - `:append` - Append to file
   - `:match-pattern` - Search for files matching pattern
   - `:read-directory` - Read directory to find files
   - `:read-metadata` - Read file metadata (test operators)
   
   ## Context Flags
   
   - `:from-substitution` - Operation from command substitution
   - `:loop-context` - Operation in loop body
   - `:conditional` - Operation in conditional branch
   - `:test-condition` - Operation in test expression
   - `:pattern-source` - Link to pattern producer command
   ```

6. Run comprehensive compatibility test suite:
   ```bash
   # Test all existing functionality still works
   make test-bash-parser
   
   # Test new recursive features
   make test TEST=test-bash-parser-recursive
   make test TEST=test-pattern-flow
   make test TEST=test-loop-context
   make test TEST=test-conditional-context
   make test TEST=test-heredoc-context
   
   # Validate corpus expectations
   emacs --batch -l config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.el \
         -f jf/test-corpus-file-ops-validate
   ```

Design rationale: The recursive analyzer is a significant architectural change, so backward compatibility is critical. Existing code using jf/bash-extract-file-operations should continue to work, but now get richer results with context flags. The main entry point signature doesn't change - it just returns more information.

The feature detection function allows code to check for new capabilities before relying on them. The documentation update ensures users understand the new features and how to interpret the enhanced output.

This completes the implementation by wiring all pieces together and ensuring the system works end-to-end.

Verification:
- Run: make test-bash-parser
- Verify: All existing tests pass without modification
- Verify: test-corpus-file-operations.el tests pass
- Test: New recursive tests pass (substitutions, loops, conditionals)
- Test: Feature detection functions return true for all features
- Confirm: Documentation reflects new capabilities
- Test: Simple commands still return simple results (backward compatible)
- Test: Complex commands return rich results with context flags

title: Fix pipeline redirection extraction
description:
While implementing file operations extraction tests (emacs-5rst), discovered that redirections in pipeline commands are not being extracted properly.

**Issue:** When a pipeline has a redirection (e.g., 'grep pattern > output.txt'), the redirection operation should be extracted but is not.

**Affected test:**
- test-extraction-pipeline-with-multiple-file-operations

**Expected behavior:**
```elisp
(jf/bash-extract-file-operations
  (jf/bash-parse "cat file.txt | grep pattern > output.txt"))
;; Should return 2 operations:
;; 1. Read from file.txt (cat command)
;; 2. Write to output.txt (grep redirection)
```

**Current behavior:** Only extracting read operation from cat, missing write operation from grep redirection.

**Investigation needed:**
- Check if parser is capturing redirections in pipeline commands
- Check if extraction logic processes redirections for each pipeline command
- May be related to how pipeline commands are parsed and stored

**Files to check:**
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.el (pipeline parsing and redirection extraction)

**Context:** specs/bash-file-operations/spec.md § 'Extract operations from redirections' + 'Handle multi-command constructs'

title: Bounds filtering includes partial overlaps despite docstring claiming exclusion
description:
**Bug**: The `jf/gptel--filter-bounds-before-position` function includes regions that start before but end after the branch point, contradicting its own docstring.

**Location**: `config/gptel/sessions/branching.el:69-87`

**Discrepancy**:
- **Docstring (line 72)**: "Regions that start before but end after POSITION are EXCLUDED (partial overlap)"
- **Implementation (line 82)**: Only checks `(< (car region) position)` - does NOT check end position

**Example**:
```
Branch point: 5420
Region: (4000 6000) - starts at 4000, ends at 6000

Expected (per docstring): EXCLUDE (partial overlap)
Actual behavior: INCLUDE (only start checked)
```

**Impact**: 
- Branch bounds may include conversation elements that extend past the branch point
- Could lead to unexpected content in branched sessions
- Violates principle of clean truncation at branch point

**Suggested Fix**:
Add end-position check to match docstring:
```elisp
(lambda (region)
  ;; Include only if region ENDS before branch point
  (< (cadr region) position))
```

Or update docstring if current behavior is intended.

**Discovered During**: OpenSpec sessions-branching spec verification

title: org-roam validation uses underscored node_ids key

title: Pattern matching corpus tests failing
description:
Two pattern matching corpus tests are failing:

1. test-corpus-match-pattern-003: Pattern matching test case failing
2. test-corpus-match-pattern-007: Another pattern matching test case failing

Pattern matching is used to identify glob patterns in commands (e.g., *.txt, file?.log, [abc]*.sh). These patterns need to be distinguished from literal filenames to properly track file operations.

Files to investigate:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-semantics.el (pattern detection)
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser-file-ops.el (glob pattern handling)

Implementation steps:
1. Identify what specific patterns are being tested in cases 003 and 007
2. Check if the pattern detection logic properly recognizes:
   - Asterisk wildcards (*.txt)
   - Question mark wildcards (file?.log)
   - Character classes ([abc]*.sh, [0-9]*.dat)
   - Brace expansion ({file1,file2}.txt)
3. Verify pattern metadata is properly attached to AST nodes
4. Ensure patterns are distinguished from literal paths

Design rationale: Proper pattern detection is critical because:
- Patterns represent sets of files, not single files
- Analysis must treat patterns differently from literal paths
- Pattern matching affects scope and file operation tracking
- Incorrect pattern detection leads to false positives/negatives in file operation analysis

Verification:
- Run make test-bash-parser
- Confirm test-corpus-match-pattern-003 and 007 pass
- Test various glob pattern formats (wildcards, character classes, brace expansion)
- Verify patterns are correctly distinguished from literal filenames with special chars

title: Fix incorrect scope-core dependency comment in gptel.org
description:
Line 239 of config/gptel/gptel.org incorrectly states that scope-core depends on scope-profiles. Analysis shows:

1. scope-core.el requires: gptel-session-constants, gptel-session-logging, yaml
2. scope-profiles.el requires: cl-lib, yaml, gptel-session-constants, gptel-session-logging
3. scope-core.el does NOT require or use scope-profiles
4. scope-profiles.el does NOT require or use scope-core

The comment should be removed or corrected to indicate that scope-profiles is loaded before scope-core for organizational reasons, not dependency reasons.

title: Main Extraction Function
description:
Implement main entry point coordinating extraction from all sources.

Files to modify:
- config/experiments/bash-parser/bash-parser.org (new section)

Implementation steps:
1. Create new org section "File Operations Extraction - Main"
2. Implement `jf/bash-extract-file-operations` public API function
3. Accept parsed-command and optional :var-context parameter
4. Coordinate extraction from all sources:
   - Call jf/bash-extract-operations-from-redirections
   - Call jf/bash-extract-operations-from-positional-args
   - Call jf/bash-extract-operations-from-exec-blocks
5. Handle multi-command constructs (pipelines, chains):
   - Detect :type :pipeline or :chain
   - Recursively extract from each command
   - Combine operations from all commands
6. Deduplicate operations (same file + operation type)
7. Assign overall confidence levels
8. Return complete list of operation plists

Design rationale:
Single entry point simplifies API - caller doesn't need to know extraction sources. Handles all command types (simple, pipeline, chain) transparently. Deduplication prevents duplicate entries when file appears multiple times. Confidence levels enable fail-safe security policies. Variable context parameter prepares for future variable resolution integration.

Design pattern:
```elisp
(defun jf/bash-extract-file-operations (parsed-command &optional var-context)
  "Extract all file operations from PARSED-COMMAND. Main entry point."
  (let ((operations nil))
    (pcase (plist-get parsed-command :type)
      (:simple-command
       (setq operations
             (append (jf/bash-extract-operations-from-redirections parsed-command)
                     (jf/bash-extract-operations-from-positional-args parsed-command)
                     (jf/bash-extract-operations-from-exec-blocks parsed-command))))
      (:pipeline
       (dolist (cmd (plist-get parsed-command :commands))
         (setq operations (append operations (jf/bash-extract-file-operations cmd var-context)))))
      (:chain
       (dolist (cmd (plist-get parsed-command :commands))
         (setq operations (append operations (jf/bash-extract-file-operations cmd var-context))))))
    ;; Deduplicate: same file + operation
    (jf/bash--deduplicate-operations operations)))
```

Verification:
- Simple commands extract from all sources
- "cat file.txt | grep pattern > output.txt" extracts from both commands in pipeline
- "rm temp.txt && touch new.txt" extracts from both commands in chain
- Duplicate operations removed (same file + operation appears once)
- Test with all multi-command scenarios from bash-file-operations/spec.md

Context: design.md § "Decision 2: File Operation Extraction Architecture"

title: [HIGH] PersistentAgent: tools.org file never created during agent initialization
